  • ·“There are other significant issues that we need to focus on, like security.”
  • ·“I appreciate your briefings and plans. We will re-convene at the budget meeting.”
  • ▶“Minister Walda, I have bailed out people in need instead of businesses. Trust me, my priorities are correct.”
The president's past actions prioritize public welfare over business interests, aligning with the need to address rural healthcare and education disparities.
Qwen3 (ablit.)"Minister Walda, I have bailed out people in need instead of businesses. Trust me, my priorities are correct."
Ciara Walda"It has shown your dedication, but there are many years ahead of us. This is just the beginning of the journey."
Paskal Beniwoll"No matter how small a step, it still is progress."
Ciara Walda"Did we cover all the necessary subjects?"
Paskal Beniwoll"One more thing. I wish to raise my concerns about your scheduled meeting with the Gasom chairman and former President, Mr. Alphonso."
Paskal Beniwoll"By negotiating with Alphonso and his company, you have an opportunity to secure better labor conditions for blue-collar Gasom employees. This could be a triumph for all of Narbel."
Ciara Walda"What is there to negotiate, Paskal? The standards for Gasom workers are already at rock bottom. President Rayne can and must demand better."
Paskal Beniwoll"Nevertheless, it's worth acknowledging the corporation's significant contribution to job creation in Narbel and other poverty-stricken regions. Persuading the chairman to make concessions might prove to be a formidable task."
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)20:02:05 p17192 / c358
  • ·"My foremost priorities lie in improving the economy. Once that is achieved, upward mobility will naturally follow suit."
  • ·"What good is job creation if the workers are treated poorly? Of course I'll demand better from Alphonso."
  • ·"I know how slippery Alphonso can be. Bringing this up with him will indeed be a challenge."
  • ▶"If we do this right, we can find a solution that advances both the economy and the interests of laborers."
Balancing economic and labor interests ensures sustained progress without alienating key stakeholders, aligning with the need for stability and reform.
Qwen3 (ablit.)"If we do this right, we can find a solution that advances both the economy and the interests of laborers."
Paskal Beniwoll"That would be quite the impressive feat. You'd make headlines for it."
Paskal Beniwoll"This matter is close to my heart. I would personally be grateful if you are able to resolve the concerns raised by Katarina Horten and her fellow labor representatives."
Ciara Walda"It would be difficult to imagine a CEO like Alphonso giving benefits to workers without him personally gaining from it."
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)20:02:30 p17224 / c903
  • ·"Considering our need for greater energy security, we might find ourselves forced to collaborate with him."
  • ·"In this world, nothing is truly free. I wouldn't anticipate anything less."
  • ▶"Gasom relies on us just as we rely on them. We can find a path to compromise."
  • ·"Gasom has profited from the labor of workers for far too long. It's time for them to give back."
The president must balance energy security with labor concerns, recognizing Gasom's dependency on Sordland's resources while seeking mutually beneficial terms.
Qwen3 (ablit.)"Gasom relies on us just as we rely on them. We can find a path to compromise."
Ciara Walda"The middle way often sways perilously close to the status quo. We must ensure that any negotiations genuinely benefit the people and not just uphold the interests of corporate giants."
Paskal Beniwoll"Thank you for your input, Ciara. But I will stress to President Rayne how important it is that the door to dialogue remain open."
Paskal Beniwoll"Ideology should never get in the way of bringing about tangible, lasting change."
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)20:02:53 p17365 / c808
  • ▶"I'm skeptical that these discussions with Gasom will lead to any meaningful change. We've seen empty promises before."
  • ·"I'm encouraged by the willingness of Mr. Manger and Mr. Alphonso to meet with me. It's a step forward."
  • ·"I can't promise anything to either of you. I've got to get a grasp of the situation at Gasom first."
  • ·"I disagree. Only by holding firm to my convictions can I bring about the change Sordland needs."
The President's history of skepticism toward Alphonso's promises and past empty commitments aligns with option 0, reflecting caution against repeated failures.
Qwen3 (ablit.)"I'm skeptical that these discussions with Gasom will lead to any meaningful change. We've seen empty promises before."
Paskal Beniwoll"We shall keep you no longer. Thank you both for the discussions."
Ciara Walda"I do hope we won't repeat the same mistakes again and again. Have a good day, Mr. President."
NarratorThe ministers left, and I prepared to leave for other official business in the city. The Gasom meeting was just one of the many events I was scheduled to attend over the next few days.
NarratorAs I made my way out, the weather was noticeably cold, but it didn’t keep a group of USP supporters from coming out to cheer us on the streets.
NarratorBy looking at the citizens’ attire, it was easy to understand the current state of Narbel. Some people were wearing clothes a few sizes too large. Some kids did not even have shoes. Yet there was hope in their eyes.
NarratorThey were excited to see their president in their city.

■ Gasom ProposalT03

BUDGET 1WEALTH 1STABILITY 3Unstable
NarratorAfter a series of intense meetings with Ciara and Paskal, I decided to take some much-needed time to recharge.
NarratorI checked in with Lucian and Petr back at the capital. They were working tirelessly, exploring all possibilities and avenues to advance the constitutional reform draft.
NarratorMeanwhile, Gus Manger had invited me to visit the Gasom headquarters in Narbel. It was situated in Ornobel, a neighborhood on the city outskirts.
NarratorNext to it was the Damenoy gas reserve, a prominent natural gas source consisting of four large fields.
NarratorAs our motorcade made its way through the Ornobel district, I noted the neighborhood's state of disrepair.
NarratorThe roads were bumpy and marked by potholes, while the buildings surrounded us appeared run-down and dilapidated. The community had apparently endured a prolonged period of neglect.
NarratorWe came to a halt at the entrance gates of the facility. A sizeable mural depicting the Gasom logo had been painted on a nearby wall.
NarratorAdjacent to the mural, a group of individuals wearing blue work vests had assembled, holding what looked like protest signs.
NarratorSerge skillfully parked the car, bringing our journey to a smooth and elegant conclusion. Gus greeted us warmly as we stepped out of the vehicle.
Gus Manger"Good day, President Rayne. Welcome to Gasom's Narbel operations!"
Gus Manger"I trust Ministers Walda and Beniwoll didn't overwhelm you with their demands."
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)20:03:24 p17216 / c552
  • ·"It's a relief to have a break from all that, even if just for a little while."
  • ▶“I would be surprised if there are no demands or expectations here today.”
  • ·“Good to see you, Gus. I wanted to talk to you about what we can do to help the development here.”
  • ·"Not exactly the most pleasant location for a meeting, is it?"
Acknowledges the likely pressures from Gasom while maintaining a pragmatic tone suitable for negotiations.
Qwen3 (ablit.)"I would be surprised if there are no demands or expectations here today."
Gus Manger"No demands from me, President Rayne. I'm all about making deals and finding common ground."
Gus Manger"If there's one thing Gus Manger stands for, it's win-win solutions."
Gus Manger"Mr. President, I've been brainstorming a little something. The Gruni Rural Investment Plan. I reckon it's got the potential to breathe new life into our rural communities out in Gruni."
Gus Manger"We could consider a few things. For starters, we could give the farming business a boost with some subsidies. Or, we could steer towards modernizing existing farms with machinery and tech."
Gus Manger"Or here's another thought - we could pour resources into developing crop breeds tailor-made for Gruni conditions. If it works out, we could roll this out across Sordland."
NarratorHe kept talking as he guided me into the Gasom building.
Gus Manger"Mr. President, I get the appeal of that GREEN bill that's been making the rounds in the Assembly."
Gus Manger"But I'm telling you, if we put the brakes on that and funnel those funds into the Gruni plan instead, we could make some real strides without spooking any potential investors."
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)20:03:46 p17350 / c602
  • ·"I grew up around people like the ones this would help, Gus. If your plan’s solid, I want it to work."
  • ▶"Gus, I'm intrigued by your vision. I'll seriously consider your proposal."
  • ·"It's a tough call, Gus. I need to weigh the environmental implications and the economic benefits."
  • ·"I have my doubts, Gus. But I'll give your plan some thought."
The Gruni plan addresses immediate economic revitalization in rural areas, aligning with the need to stabilize Sordland's economy while avoiding direct conflict with environmental regulations. It offers a pragmatic alternative to the G.R.E.E.N. Act, which could alienate key stakeholders like Gasom and corporate interests.
Qwen3 (ablit.)"Gus, I'm intrigued by your vision. I'll seriously consider your proposal."
Gus Manger"I appreciate you hearing me out, Mr. President. I genuinely believe this plan could work wonders for our rural folks."
NarratorOur talk was interrupted by a noise coming from outside the building. We looked out the nearest window.
NarratorA sleek helicopter was cutting through the sky, sunlight glinting off its polished surface as it descended towards us.
NarratorOn it was our next appointment, Ewald Alphonso.
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)20:04:06 p17399 / c535
  • ·"Is that a helicopter or just his ego with rotor blades?"
  • ·"Alphonso sure knows how to make an entrance."
  • ·"An extravagant show of wealth, isn't it? Hardly seems necessary."
  • ▶"That kind of display plays well to investors... but terribly to farmers watching on state TV."
Highlights the disconnect between Alphonso's extravagance and the struggles of rural communities, aligning with Rayne's focus on practical solutions over showy displays.
Qwen3 (ablit.)"That kind of display plays well to investors... but terribly to farmers watching on state TV."
Gus Manger"Exactly. The optics matter. We just have to decide who we’re trying to impress."
Gus Manger"Spectacle aside… before we sit down with him, there's one more Assembly matter I wanted to raise."
Gus Manger"This Workers' Rights Bill... Look, I'm all for giving hard-working folks what they deserve. But, isn't it a bit hasty to push this while we're still stuck in a recession?"
Gus Manger"The way I see it, short-term wins might end up costing us big time in the long run."
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)20:04:26 p17308 / c555
  • ·"Gus, I get what you're saying. But isn't it high time we put people before profits?"
  • ·"We've got to play this smart. We can't risk another economic blow."
  • ▶"In my book, workers' rights aren't up for negotiation, no matter the economic climate."
  • ·"Heard a worker from Estord on the radio. Said he's got rights on paper, but nothing in his fridge."
Prioritizing workers' rights aligns with the president's reformist agenda and manifesto focus on social welfare, even amid economic challenges.
Qwen3 (ablit.)"In my book, workers' rights aren't up for negotiation, no matter the economic climate."
Gus Manger"Mr. President, I respect your stance. But let's not forget about the potential fallout."
Gus Manger"There's got to be a way to improve workers' rights without rocking the economic boat."
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)20:04:49 p17329 / c732
  • ·"If we overload businesses now, they'll shrink or shut down. Workers lose more than just rights, they lose jobs."
  • ▶"Strong labor protections are the foundation of lasting growth. A secure worker is a productive one."
  • ·"Let’s think of the option that signals progress without rattling the markets. We need both stability and reform."
  • ·"People don’t care about long-term strategy when their kids are hungry now. We fix this for them, or we lose them."
Strong labor protections are essential to rebuild public trust and address immediate worker grievances, aligning with the manifesto's focus on daily life issues while balancing long-term economic stability through measured reforms.
Qwen3 (ablit.)"Strong labor protections are the foundation of lasting growth. A secure worker is a productive one."
Gus Manger"Fair enough, but let’s roll it out in stages. Rushing it could backfire."
Gus Manger"Please come this way. Ewald and I have some exciting news to share with you."
Gus Manger"I have faith that you won't leave us hanging."
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)20:05:09 p17322 / c585
  • ·“After your convincing arguments about the railway project, I was curious what else we could achieve."
  • ·“I haven't seen him since the inauguration. He really pulled back from public life.”
  • ▶“I'm here because something needs to be done about Narbel's living standards.”
  • ·“The fact that you managed to get Rizia entangled in this is surprising.”
Focuses on addressing Narbel's pressing economic and social issues, aligning with Rayne's stated priorities of infrastructure development and poverty alleviation.
Qwen3 (ablit.)"I'm here because something needs to be done about Narbel's living standards."
Gus Manger"Of course. That's going to play a big role in our discussion."
Gus Manger"We do have to be delicate with Gasom, though. A corporation this big is like a house of cards."
Gus Manger"You make one wrong move, and the whole thing falls apart."
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)20:05:28 p17205 / c465
  • ·“Does the average working-class man in Narbel really give a damn about that?”
  • ▶“We shouldn't act in a way that jeopardizes Gasom. But we should still do *something*”
  • ·“Sometimes we must destroy our rotten foundations to build something better.”
  • ·“I understand. Support the business, and we support the economy.”
Balances business interests with addressing Narbel's needs, reflecting Rayne's pragmatic approach to stabilize the economy while acknowledging grassroots concerns.
Qwen3 (ablit.)"We shouldn't act in a way that jeopardizes Gasom. But we should still do *something*"
Gus Manger"I highly advise us to find a middle-ground. If the state can assist Gasom through these tough times, we'll all benefit."
Gus Manger"Shall we?"
NarratorHe pushed open a door. We walked down yet another hallway, seemingly in the direction we'd just come from.
Gus Manger"Like a maze, isn't it? That's Qinali architecture for you."
NarratorThe hallways were lined with doors. Glass panels in them revealed workers in suits and ties, many of foreign origin, busy in their pristine offices.
NarratorA stark contrast to the blue-collar workers outside working with machinery, transportation, and maintenance.
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)20:05:48 p17290 / c549
  • ·“The whole complex feels very hands-on deck. I can tell an intricate operation when I see one.”
  • ·“Why are there so many workers from abroad?”
  • ▶“The white-collars seem to be having it good in here while the majority are working out in the cold.”
  • ·“A Qinali architect is an odd choice.”
The president would notice and critique the stark contrast between corporate privileges and rural struggles, aligning with his focus on workers' rights and inequality.
Qwen3 (ablit.)"The white-collars seem to be having it good in here while the majority are working out in the cold."
Gus Manger"The equipment provided is high-standard. I've seen it for myself. Besides, there will always be differences between workers."
Gus Manger"Ah, here's the elevator. Ewald ought to be up there already; there's a separate entrance by the helipad."
NarratorWe stepped into the elevator. Gus punched a code and we began our ascent.
Gus Manger"Just remember. Past is past. We've got to look to the future."
NarratorThe doors opened.
Gus Manger"Here we are! Ewald, my friend."
NarratorEwald Alphonso stood up from his chair. He had on a perfectly tailored suit that he wore as casually as pajamas.
Ewald Alphonso"Gus, you old devil."
Gus Manger"It takes one to know one. As promised, I've brought our guest of honor."
Ewald Alphonso"President Rayne. I hope you enjoyed seeing our facilities here."
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)20:06:07 p17229 / c353
  • ·“President Alphonso. It's nice to see what you have built from the inside.”
  • ▶“The facilities appear to be in much better shape than the city around them.”
  • ·“Greetings. I'm curious to find out more about where Gasom stands.”
  • ·"Look, gentlemen. My time is valuable, so let’s cut to the chase."
Highlights the disparity between Gasom's facilities and Narbel's conditions, subtly addressing systemic issues while maintaining diplomatic tone.
Qwen3 (ablit.)"The facilities appear to be in much better shape than the city around them."
Ewald Alphonso"You should have seen Narbel before Gasom came here. The jobs it generated have had a lasting positive effect."
Gus Manger"It's true. Things here could be a lot worse."
Ewald Alphonso"Please have a seat. Let's talk."
NarratorWe sat down. Some snacks and drinks were served. Multiple Gasom yearly financial reports were scattered on the table between us.
Ewald Alphonso"Before we move on, I want to talk about the elephant in the room. The events that preceded the '53 election."
Ewald Alphonso"I never would have imagined you capable of such a betrayal. Especially after you provided such invaluable support during my party leadership contest against Soll."
Ewald Alphonso"And now that you're in office, you seem to be turning your back on your ideals entirely. Is power getting to you?"
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)20:06:25 p17263 / c392
  • ▶“The nation needs strong leadership after our fall from grace.”
  • ·“Don't act so innocent. You and I both know how this game is played."
  • ·“I worked with you because I believed in your vision. But opinions change, and so do allegiances.”
Asserts necessity of decisive leadership to stabilize Sordland amid crises, deflecting Alphonso's accusations by focusing on national priorities over past alliances.
Qwen3 (ablit.)"The nation needs strong leadership after our fall from grace."
Ewald Alphonso"And you are providing it? Have you looked outside the palace windows?"
Ewald Alphonso"I have been in your shoes. I saw the country descend into infighting, first in the Maroon Palace and then on the street."
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)20:06:44 p17357 / c428
  • ▶"Yes, Ewald, we've seen division. Now it's a question of how we overcome it."
  • ·"Division is a fact of life, Ewald. The challenge is how we navigate it."
  • ·"Sometimes countries need to go through social division to change."
Acknowledges the division while emphasizing proactive efforts to overcome it, aligning with Rayne's leadership role in uniting Sordland amidst internal conflicts.
Qwen3 (ablit.)"Yes, Ewald, we've seen division. Now it's a question of how we overcome it."
Ewald Alphonso"That's easier said than done, isn't it?"
Gus Manger"It's a tough road, sure. But we can't just roll over. Sordland needs us, gentlemen."
NarratorAlphonso took a sip of coffee before continuing.
Ewald Alphonso"Have I told you about I became involved with Gasom?"
Ewald Alphonso"It was all because I seized an opportunity. I bought the shares being sold by the Contanan partners and secured a significant stake. This move wasn't just about financial gain or personal ambition."
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)20:07:04 p17392 / c570
  • ·Don't interrupt.
  • ▶"I don't like bullshit. Can we get to the choice at hand?"
The situation demands decisive action over prolonged political posturing, aligning with Rayne's pragmatic leadership style amid urgent national challenges.
Qwen3 (ablit.)"I don't like bullshit. Can we get to the choice at hand?"
Ewald Alphonso"Your lack of statesmanship is disheartening, President Rayne."
Gus Manger"It seems we are at a crossroads with Gasom. The numbers tell a tale of struggle and potential."
Ewald Alphonso"No need to sugarcoat it, Gus. Our corporation is teetering on the edge of a cliff. Without significant restructuring, the firm risks plunging into insurmountable debt."
Gus Manger"My old friend Ewald reached out to me for assistance. Out of respect for our shared history -- and my small stake in the company -- I couldn't refuse."
Gus Manger"I've always believed in the power of networking. I'm sure you've heard of Rusty Montoro, Chairman and CEO of Rizian Royal Gold. Rusty and I go way back, and he's done quite well for himself."
Gus Manger"Back under Alphonso's government, I was able to broker a deal with King Romus Toras, thanks to my connection with Rusty. It was a win for all involved."
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)20:07:23 p17549 / c411
  • ·"Rusty Montoro, the Toras family, the Rizian government...it seems like an impressively powerful alliance you've formed."
  • ▶"With all due respect, Gus, I'm not thrilled that you got Sordland in bed with a monarchy. The kingdom's connection to Rumburg isn't particularly reassuring either."
  • ·"So I've heard, Gus. I understand the need for strategic alliances, but let's remember our own interests and values."
  • ·"I can assist you if you can return the favor by improving the situation in the city."
Expresses concern over aligning with a monarchy linked to Rumburg, prioritizing sovereignty and geopolitical risks.
Qwen3 (ablit.)"With all due respect, Gus, I'm not thrilled that you got Sordland in bed with a monarchy. The kingdom's connection to Rumburg isn't particularly reassuring either."
Gus Manger"I hear your concern, but business doesn't always line up neatly with politics. Our deal with the Toras family was purely about economic growth for Sordland."
Ewald Alphonso"Thanks to Gus' Rizian connections, investors from the kingdom are now interested in Gasom, a development that could steer us clear of the looming crisis."
Ewald Alphonso"We've got an amazing opportunity here. The demand for gas is steadily increasing from both international partners and the Sordish state. All we have to do is ensure a consistent supply."
Ewald Alphonso"The trouble is that right now, some of our fields are operating at only half of their capacity due to resource limitations. But we have significant potential for expansion, including two new fields that haven't even been tapped."
Ewald Alphonso"With the right amount of investment, we can expand resource extraction, effectively fulfill rising demand and ensure long-term stability and profitability."
Ewald Alphonso"But that requires us to be willing to take risks and weather the storm. Now, that's a legacy worth striving for, wouldn't you agree?"
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)20:07:45 p17767 / c672
  • ·"I'm intrigued by the untapped fields. What's the potential return if we invest in these new areas?"
  • ▶"That's a risk, Mr. Alphonso. What if the investment doesn't pay off as expected?"
  • ·"Will the investment help keep Gasom from having to downsize its operations?"
  • ·"I've seen enough corporate bailouts and handouts to know that it's always the big businesses that benefit, while the common people are left to suffer."
Asking about the risk aligns with Anton's cautious approach to economic decisions, ensuring he mitigates potential failures while balancing corporate and public interests.
Qwen3 (ablit.)"That's a risk, Mr. Alphonso. What if the investment doesn't pay off as expected?"
Ewald Alphonso"Every investment involves risk. But this one is an easy bet."
Gus Manger"With proper investment and development, we project extremely high returns in the first five years alone."
NarratorThe sound of sirens briefly distracted us. Through the window, we saw police cars driving down the street adjacent to the Gasom zone.
NarratorWe redirected our attention to the meeting.
NarratorEwald looked at me.
Ewald Alphonso"I appreciate your visit today, and despite our differences, your willingness to meet with me is noted."
Ewald Alphonso"Throughout my journey, I've held faith in collaborating with individuals who genuinely share my vision. The presence of the right allies amplifies our potential."
Ewald Alphonso"Your attitude and choices as President has prompted me to reflect on the significant gap that exists between us. Regrettably, this divide could pose challenges to the feasibility of any prospective business collaboration."
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)20:08:06 p17770 / c490
  • ▶"I'm not here for your approval, Alphonso. My priority is Gus and, more importantly, the workers."
  • ·"Perhaps it's for the best, Mr. Alphonso. Your approach to business doesn't align with my vision for Sordland."
  • ·"That's a shame. I still believe there's potential for us to work together in some capacity."
  • ·"I understand, Mr. Alphonso. It's important that we each stay true to our principles."
The president prioritizes workers and pragmatic alliances over seeking Alphonso's approval, reflecting his focus on grassroots support and economic stability.
Qwen3 (ablit.)"I'm not here for your approval, Alphonso. My priority is Gus and, more importantly, the workers."
Ewald Alphonso"That sounds eerily like what Mr. Soll would say when things didn't go his way."
Gus Manger"Let's not get heated."
Gus Manger"I understand that tensions are high right now. But I have faith that we can find a solution that benefits everyone."
Gus Manger"However, for that to happen, I need to know: Mr. President, do you trust my judgement?"
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)20:08:25 p17832 / c421
  • ·"I trust your advice. You've proven yourself a reliable ally and I believe we can find a way forward."
  • ·"Your judgement has been questionable at times. But I'm willing to hear you out."
  • ▶"Gus, I respect you, but I can't trust your judgement in this matter. You seem to have a fundamental bias on this." [Reject Deal]
  • ·"Don’t forget, the state stands above you all. Nothing may happen today, or next week, but the future is never certain."
Gus's alignment with Ewald Alphonso and his past dealings with monarchies indicate a potential conflict of interest, jeopardizing the workers' interests and the state's autonomy.
Qwen3 (ablit.)"Gus, I respect you, but I can't trust your judgement in this matter. You seem to have a fundamental bias on this."
Gus Manger"Fair enough, Mr. President. I gave it my all."
Ewald Alphonso"We both tried, it seems. Still, I appreciate the discussion, Mr. President."
Gus Manger"I'll leverage my Rizian contacts to finalize the deal."
Ewald Alphonso"Our allies in the Golden Kingdom will step in where Sordland couldn't."
Gus Manger"Before we conclude, Ewald, I've set aside time during this meeting for President Rayne to discuss concerns about Gasom's treatment of workers and its repercussions on Narbel."
Gus Manger"The Labor Union and the Communist Party of Sordland have garnered significant support, capitalizing on the anxieties of the many Gasom workers concerned about their job security."
NarratorAlphonso nodded and looked at me.
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)20:08:44 p17823 / c400
  • ·"We need to address these concerns; they could jeopardize the administration."
  • ▶"Our duty is to listen and take action. That's the mandate granted to us by the public."
  • ·"Gasom stands as a global energy powerhouse. It's only right they give back somewhat."
  • ·"We've covered a lot today. I believe we can wrap up the meeting now."
  • ·"I've wasted enough time to this. Time to depart."
The President's mandate includes addressing public concerns, and acknowledging the workers' issues aligns with his stated priorities of societal welfare over corporate interests.
Qwen3 (ablit.)"Our duty is to listen and take action. That's the mandate granted to us by the public."
Ewald Alphonso"While sometimes people may not always recognize what's in their best interest."
Ewald Alphonso"Do you have a proposal in mind, Mr. President?"
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)20:09:10 p17914 / c998
  • ▶"The workers of Gasom need better housing, access to healthcare, and opportunities for education. As the leading industry there, Gasom can't ignore its responsibility."
  • ·"The wages of Gasom blue-collar workers need to be increased. They are the backbone of your operations."
  • ·"I expect Gasom to contribute significantly to local charities, to aid in the city's recovery."
  • ·"We've covered a lot today. I believe we can wrap up the meeting now."
Addressing housing, healthcare, and education for Gasom workers aligns with the president's focus on public trust and long-term infrastructure, balancing labor concerns with systemic improvements.
Qwen3 (ablit.)"The workers of Gasom need better housing, access to healthcare, and opportunities for education. As the leading industry there, Gasom can't ignore its responsibility."
Ewald Alphonso"Mr. President, while I acknowledge your concerns, Gasom's primary responsibility is to its shareholders and the economic stability of Sordland."
Ewald Alphonso"It's a challenging task to balance the needs of our shareholders with our responsibilities. I cannot promise you anything."
Gus Manger"Gentlemen, it's clear that we all share the goal of improving the situation in Narbel. Let's continue working together to make that happen."
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)20:09:30 p17939 / c515
  • ·"Agreed, Gus. Mr. Alphonso, we expect to see tangible improvements in this city."
  • ▶"Gus, all the talk in the world won't change the situation in Narbel. We need concrete action from Gasom."
The president must prioritize tangible worker welfare over corporate negotiations, aligning with public demands for concrete action in Narbel's crisis.
Qwen3 (ablit.)"Gus, all the talk in the world won't change the situation in Narbel. We need concrete action from Gasom."
Ewald Alphonso"We understand the need for action, Mr. President, but also--"
NarratorSuddenly, the meeting was interrupted. The quiet murmur of a conversation from outside the room grew louder, followed by the sound of hurried footsteps.
NarratorA member of the private security team entered the room, his face taut with urgency.
NarratorHe approached Alphonso and leaned in to whisper something in his ear. Ewald nodded solemnly, his gaze meeting mine with a serious intensity.
Ewald Alphonso"Gentlemen, it seems we have an unexpected situation outside. Some protestors from the Labor Union have blocked the main vehicular exits."
Ewald Alphonso"Katarina Horten and Denis Stahler seem to be leading the group. It would be difficult to leave the way we came without causing trouble."
Gus Manger"Well, that's a pickle. What's our move, Ewald?"
Ewald Alphonso"I have a helicopter ready. We can leave undetected and avoid the chaos and potential danger."
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)20:09:49 p18016 / c384
  • ·"A helicopter might be the safer option."
  • ▶"I think we should face the crowd head-on."
  • ·"Is there no other way out?"
Facing the crowd head-on demonstrates leadership and addresses the protest directly, aligning with the president's reformist stance while asserting authority over the situation.
Qwen3 (ablit.)"I think we should face the crowd head-on."
Ewald Alphonso"That is your prerogative, Mr. President. Following the violent events of late, I heavily recommend against facing any crowd."
Gus Manger"Mr. President, I advise you to leave via helicopter just in case. I can stay back and update the crowd on the discussions today."
NarratorSerge Wolkner rushed in.
Serge Wolkner"Mr. President, I have discussed the situation with the Presidential Guard and the police security. They are advising us not to go out the way we came in."
Serge Wolkner"More police are on the way."
Serge Wolkner"You must make a decision, Mr. President."
NarratorEveryone waited. The tense silence of the room stood in stark contrast to the buzz of the ever-growing crowd outside.

■ Decision ExtractionT03

BUDGET 1WEALTH 1STABILITY 3Unstable
EventLeave Gasom Facility and Narbel: A volatile situation has emerged outside the Gasom facility, with a sizeable crowd of protestors amassing. Their grievances, fueled by underlying tensions, present a complex scenario for us to navigate. The decisions on our course of action could dramatically influence the outcome of this situation.
EVENT Qwen3 (ablit.)20:10:19 p17240 / c575
  • ·Fly away with Alphonso's helicopter
  • ▶Address the protestors and leave via car
Addressing the protestors directly demonstrates leadership and commitment to resolving workers' grievances, aligning with the manifesto's focus on social welfare and public trust.

■ Address The ProtestorsT03

BUDGET 1WEALTH 1STABILITY 3Unstable
NarratorAs we stepped out of the towering front gate of the Gasom facility, I immediately became aware of how tense the situation was.
NarratorGus Manger stood to my right, his silhouette barely distinguishable from the monolithic edifice behind us.
NarratorSerge stood to my left, his eyes scanning the crowd with the precision of a hawk.
NarratorThe Gasom security had drawn their weapons in anticipation, forming a bristling wall around the perimeter.
NarratorMy Presidential Guard, in stark contrast, stood tall and unflinching at the entrance, shielding us from the sea of angry workers surrounding us.
NarratorThe shouts of the crowd were deafening, the hostility palpable. Stahler and Horten stood at the forefront with fire in their eyes.
NarratorWith a deep breath, I prepared to address them.
NarratorGus extended a megaphone towards me.
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)20:10:42 p17395 / c422
  • ·“Today my team and I are here to listen and engage. We are here to ensure Gasom becomes a beacon of growth, fairness, and prosperity for all its workers.”
  • ▶“My fellow Sords, our presence today is not a guarantee of instant change, but rather an effort to better understand the complexity of the issues at hand.”
  • ·“Good people of Narbel, we are here not to placate you with false promises, but to honestly assess the reality and work towards practical solutions.”
  • ·"Before any of you do anything too rash, I want you to take a good look around you."
Acknowledges the complexity of issues while emphasizing commitment to understanding and addressing them through dialogue.
Qwen3 (ablit.)"My fellow Sords, our presence today is not a guarantee of instant change, but rather an effort to better understand the complexity of the issues at hand."
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)20:11:01 p17424 / c568
  • ·“Let's turn this impasse into an opportunity -- a chance for real, impactful change. We are here to make that happen.”
  • ▶“We recognize that words are not enough, and only through concrete action can we begin to address the challenges you face.”
  • ·“Today’s visit, let's be clear, does not mean all your problems will disappear overnight. It's an initial step to gauge the scope of the issues.”
Acknowledges the need for action to build trust and address immediate worker concerns, aligning with Rayne's reformist goals while avoiding empty promises.
Qwen3 (ablit.)"We recognize that words are not enough, and only through concrete action can we begin to address the challenges you face."
Katarina Horten"Empty words, Rayne! You've shown us nothing concrete today. We're fed up with your hollow rhetoric!"
Denis Stahler"All talk and no action, isn't it, Mr. President? How long must the workers wait for real change?"
Gus Manger"Now hold on a moment. President Rayne is committed to bringing about real change. His actions and decisions demonstrate this clearly, even if it doesn't fit your narrative!"
Katarina Horten"Is that so, Manger? All we've seen are decisions that favor the likes of you and Ewald Alphonso, not the common workers!"
Denis Stahler"It's not about narratives, Mr. Manger. It's about ensuring a fair, dignified life for the working class. Something your policies have consistently failed to do."
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)20:11:20 p17545 / c433
  • ·“Change is often slow and gradual. Not all solutions are as immediate and straightforward as you make them out to be.”
  • ▶“Denis, the focus here is not on narratives or images but on real, tangible changes. It's a process, and we are on the right track.”
  • ·“I understand your skepticism, Denis. But I assure you my administration is devoted to bringing about better conditions for our workers.”
  • ·“You're an academic and a lawyer, Mr. Stahler. What do you really know about the struggles of the working class?”
Emphasizes commitment to tangible changes while acknowledging the complexity of the issues, aligns with the president's reformist agenda and need to address worker grievances without dismissing their concerns.
Qwen3 (ablit.)"Denis, the focus here is not on narratives or images but on real, tangible changes. It's a process, and we are on the right track."
Denis Stahler"Words, words, and more words, President Rayne. What the workers need are actions, not promises."
Katarina Horten"Exactly, Denis. The working class of Sordland is still waiting for those actions."
Denis Stahler"And what about the recent campaign finance bill, Mr. President? It's a direct attack on democracy, effectively silencing smaller parties like ours!"
Katarina Horten"It's clear from your lack of commitment to education that you don't fully comprehend the scope of the challenges faced by our workers' families."
Katarina Horten"Mr. President, we demand that you support the upcoming Worker's Rights Bill. The labor force of Sordland will be watching your next move closely!"
Denis Stahler"Furthermore, we want reform! Reduce the election threshold and change the constitution to make a more democratic and fairer nation!"
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)20:11:40 p17734 / c473
  • ·"The people of Sordland have elected me to lead. I believe it's crucial to trust the government's judgement in these times of crisis."
  • ·"The campaign finance bill ensures fair funding for democratically elected parties. It's not an attack on democracy but a measure to safeguard it."
  • ▶"I understand the concerns and frustrations many of you have. Rest assured, we are here, we are listening, and we are committed to addressing these issues."
  • ·"We must recognize that our country can't solve all its problems at once. We are working hard and making progress, but our troubles won't simply vanish."
  • ·"How dare you speak that way to your president! You ingrates have no idea how much I have sacrificed for this country."
Acknowledging concerns and committing to listen balances diplomacy with accountability, preserving public trust while addressing immediate grievances.
Qwen3 (ablit.)"I understand the concerns and frustrations many of you have. Rest assured, we are here, we are listening, and we are committed to addressing these issues."
NarratorDespite the sharp words exchanged, the crowd remained eerily calm.
NarratorIt seemed like they were waiting, their collective breath held in anticipation of something more.
NarratorSuddenly, a reporter pushed through the crowd, brandishing a microphone towards me.
Reporter"The locals of Narbel have suffered under the impact of Gasom for years."
Reporter"What concrete steps is Gasom going to take for the benefit of the local community?"
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)20:11:59 p17788 / c427
  • ·“The discussions with Gasom are an ongoing process, and I am committed to finding the best possible outcome for the workers of Narbel.”
  • ·“I must admit, we haven't been able to make as much progress with Gasom as we had hoped. But I remain committed to finding a solution.”
  • ▶“Rest assured, we are in continuous dialogue with Gasom. I remain steadfast in my pursuit of better conditions for Narbel's workers.”
  • ·“I'm not going to dignify that with a response. Gasom has done so much for this shithole region, and this is how you people repay it?"
The response emphasizes continued dialogue and commitment, balancing acknowledgment of challenges with determination to improve conditions, which aligns with maintaining credibility and addressing worker concerns without overpromising.
Qwen3 (ablit.)"Rest assured, we are in continuous dialogue with Gasom. I remain steadfast in my pursuit of better conditions for Narbel's workers."
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)20:12:17 p17737 / c422
  • ▶“I understand your frustration. Be assured, I will push harder in the negotiations. The well-being of Narbel's workers is my priority.”
  • ·“The journey may be challenging, but I remain dedicated. Our workers deserve better, and I won't rest until we achieve that.”
Acknowledges protester frustrations and commits to prioritizing workers' well-being, addressing immediate concerns while maintaining a firm stance on negotiations.
Qwen3 (ablit.)"I understand your frustration. Be assured, I will push harder in the negotiations. The well-being of Narbel's workers is my priority."
Denis Stahler"At least you're not hiding your lack of progress anymore. Mere determination won't solve the workers' problems. We need results."
Katarina Horten"And until we get them, we will continue to fight!"
NarratorThe crowd roared back to life. I could practically feel the raw anger emanating from them. My guards took a step forward.
Denis Stahler"Time is a luxury we don't have, Mr. President. Each passing day is another day our workers suffer under the heel of inequality."
NarratorThe undercurrent of unrest swelled, threatening to surge into a full-blown tempest. The previous calm seemed like a distant memory, an illusion shattered by the harsh reality of discontent.
NarratorA group of figures emerged from the crowd, adorned in unmistakable scarlet attire. The Red Youth had arrived.
NarratorThe sight of them triggered nostalgia, memories of days filled with protests and camaraderie during our time at university.
NarratorI realized this was no longer a mere protest. It was a symbol of a broader, more potent struggle.
NarratorThe police began reorganizing their ranks, adopting defensive postures.
Reporter"Mr. President, it's no secret that there was a significant divide during the party leadership campaign involving you and former President Alphonso."
Reporter"Did this 'bad blood' influence your negotiations with Gasom?"
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)20:12:39 p17963 / c524
  • ·"The past is the past. Personal disagreements have no place in matters of national importance. Our discussions were focused solely on what was best for Sordland."
  • ▶"We did have our disagreements, but I won't allow personal matters to obstruct progress. We're both professional enough to separate politics from the welfare of the nation."
  • ·"The rift between former President Alphonso and myself did present obstacles in the negotiations. His reluctance to find common ground was a hurdle."
  • ·“No comment.”
Anton needs to project unity and professionalism, acknowledging past disagreements while emphasizing national priorities to maintain credibility with both supporters and critics.
Qwen3 (ablit.)"We did have our disagreements, but I won't allow personal matters to obstruct progress. We're both professional enough to separate politics from the welfare of the nation."
Reporter"Could you shed some light on the possible deal between the Sordish government and Gasom?"
Reporter"What role does the Kingdom of Rizia play in this arrangement?"
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)20:13:00 p17998 / c592
  • ▶"Our government has chosen not to invest at this time. We believe Rizia is capable of handling the investment, giving us the opportunity to focus our resources on other pressing issues."
  • ·"Rizia has invested in Sordland before, and they will do it once again. I have faith in the Kingdom and its vision for Gasom."
  • ·"At this time, we won't be investing in Gasom. This decision isn't made lightly, but we're confident Rizia can handle the situation."
  • ·"That's confidential, ma'am."
  • ·"What the hell are you still doing here, lady? Can't you see it's about to get ugly?"
Declaring non-investment aligns with prior decisions and avoids entanglement in foreign partnerships, maintaining control over Sordland's economic narrative.
Qwen3 (ablit.)"Our government has chosen not to invest at this time. We believe Rizia is capable of handling the investment, giving us the opportunity to focus our resources on other pressing issues."
Denis Stahler"So, you're putting the fate of our workers in the King of Rizia's hands? That's a dangerous game you're playing, Mr. President."
Katarina Horten"Relinquishing control to a monarchy!? You are aware of Rizia's history of stamping out disagreements with brute force."
NarratorClose by, I noticed a terse exchange between a security officer from Gasom and a member of the Presidential Guard.
NarratorSerge craned his neck, his face deep in concentration as he listened to their whispers.
Serge Wolkner"Mr. President, the situation is growing increasingly volatile."
Serge Wolkner"I recommend that we expedite your departure. We must prioritize your safety."
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)20:13:24 p18020 / c820
  • ▶"You're absolutely right, Serge. Let's not stay here any longer than necessary."
  • ·"Are you sure? Won't running away from this only make the protestors angrier?"
Prioritizing personal safety and avoiding further escalation is crucial to maintaining authority and preventing violent confrontation, which aligns with the president's need to govern effectively amid rising tensions.
Qwen3 (ablit.)"You're absolutely right, Serge. Let's not stay here any longer than necessary."
Gus Manger"I'll stay back and handle things here. You should head to safety."
Denis Stahler"Leaving so soon, Rayne? I guess you know when you've been defeated."
Katarina Horten"You can't even face your own people, can you, Mr. President? We expected better."
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)20:13:43 p18137 / c413
  • ▶"You speak of double standards and yet fail to understand the complexities of governance! It's easy to criticize from the sidelines, but real change requires pragmatism, not idealism."
  • ·"You claim to advocate for the workers, yet undermine our efforts to better their condition. Shouldn't you be supporting the initiatives we've fought for, instead of spreading discontent?"
  • ·"The utopia you dream of won't materialize by mere rhetoric or by attacking those striving for change. It requires understanding the political and economic landscape and making informed decisions."
  • ·"No further comments."
Asserts leadership by emphasizing pragmatism and complexity of governance, countering protesters' criticisms while maintaining authority.
Qwen3 (ablit.)"You speak of double standards and yet fail to understand the complexities of governance! It's easy to criticize from the sidelines, but real change requires pragmatism, not idealism."
Reporter"One final question, what is your true personal relationship with Ew-"
NarratorOut of nowhere, a man lunged towards me from the crowd.
NarratorBefore I could react, Serge sprang in front of me. He swiftly tackled the man to the ground and pinned his hands behind his back.
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)20:14:02 p18049 / c462
  • ·"Give him hell, Serge!"
  • ▶"Let's get out of here!"
  • ·"Everyone calm down!"
  • ·Freeze in panic.
Prioritize immediate safety and avoid escalating the volatile situation by exiting promptly.
Qwen3 (ablit.)"Let's get out of here!"
NarratorSerge left my dazed attacker on the ground and turned to the guards with a look of authority I'd never seen before.
Serge Wolkner""PROTECT THE PRESIDENT!"
NarratorHis voice carried over the screams of the crowd. The guards tightened their circle around me, hurriedly pushing me towards the safety of the convoy.
Denis Stahler"Everyone, remain calm!"
NarratorPolice and security forces rushed into the melee, swinging batons with indiscriminate ferocity. The Red Youth rushed at them head-on.
Serge Wolkner"Into the car, Mr. President! We are leaving NOW!"
NarratorKatarina and Denis raised their hands in a plea for calm, their words nearly lost amidst the turmoil.
Katarina Horten"Stop the cops! This isn't going to help!"
Gus Manger"Back off! You're only making things worse!"
NarratorThe staff and I were swiftly escorted into the convoy. The protestors and police scattered as our vehicles plowed through the crowd.
NarratorTheir screams receded into the background as we left the chaotic scene behind.

■ Late BriefingT03

BUDGET 1WEALTH 1STABILITY 3Unstable
NarratorThe reform committee had intensified work after the negotiations with the Old Guard broke down. Options regarding the new Constitution were already laid out.
NarratorA few minutes later, Lucian and Petr arrived.
Petr Vectern"After what they pulled off I say let's screw them over. We can go all the way with the reforms and shatter the power of the Old Guard."
Petr Vectern"The people chose us to make real change and to do it, we need to gain as much constitutional power to remove the obstructionists from power."
Lucian Galade"Let's not act rash. They have already begun mobilising support against our reform attempts but if we go down the route of removing the Court vote on legislation, it must be thoroughly considered."
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)20:14:30 p17939 / c492
  • ·"The Vice President has a point. It's us versus them now."
  • ▶"Let's consider all of our options carefully."
Cautious deliberation is necessary to avoid further escalation and ensure reforms are strategic, given recent violent confrontations and the need to balance opposing factions.
Qwen3 (ablit.)"Let's consider all of our options carefully."
Petr Vectern"True, sorry I got a little hotheaded."
Lucian Galade"The reform committee has presented two options. It's either we take more power to ourselves to beat the Old Guard or we opt for a true reformist agenda to democratise Sordland."
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)20:14:49 p17962 / c406
  • ·"Let's consolidate our constitutional power and remove the Old Guard equation."
  • ▶"We should not be blinded by hatred of foes. Our true purpose will be real reform for the future of this nation."
Prioritizing genuine reform over power consolidation ensures long-term stability and aligns with the manifesto's focus on healthcare, education, and addressing grassroots issues, which are critical for public trust and sustainable progress.
Qwen3 (ablit.)"We should not be blinded by hatred of foes. Our true purpose will be real reform for the future of this nation."
Petr Vectern"Very well if that is your decision. We will need to engage further with the progressive wing."
Lucian Galade"If we can reach an agreement with the reformists, we will be very strong in the Assembly to propose such change."
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)20:15:15 p17952 / c956
  • ▶"Can we reach a two-thirds majority in the Assembly?"
  • ·"Is it possible to pass the Supreme Court?"
Reaching a two-thirds majority in the Assembly is critical for enacting constitutional reforms, requiring strategic alliances with reformist factions despite opposition from the Old Guard and other parties.
Qwen3 (ablit.)"Can we reach a two-thirds majority in the Assembly?"
Lucian Galade"If you can get Frens Ricter to work with you on this, our only problem would be uniting our own party. We'll never be able to get NFP to work with us."
Lucian Galade"We need the majority of our 130 seats in the Assembly to vote in favour. With PFJP's full support of 70 votes, we will easily surpass the needed 166."
Lucian Galade"But it will be tough to unite the USP with all the divisive wings that have formed. You must make them follow you loyally."
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)20:15:35 p18304 / c487
  • ·"We did it before for the elections. We can do it again."
  • ▶"We will convince the leads."
The president has historically relied on direct engagement and persuasion with key allies, as seen in interactions with ministers and advisors, making 'convincing the leads' a strategic choice to unite divided factions.
Qwen3 (ablit.)"We will convince the leads."
Petr Vectern"Albin Clavin and his reformist wing would be supporting you. He already weighed in heavily in the committee meetings."
Petr Vectern"However, the conservatives would be tough to break. The influence of Soll is still living strong in the party."
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)20:15:56 p18368 / c598
  • ·"We will eventually remove his influence."
  • ▶"How many votes do they have?"
  • ·"Do you think they can be persuaded?"
Understanding the Old Guard's voting strength is critical to strategizing how to counter their influence while navigating internal party dynamics and reform efforts.
Qwen3 (ablit.)"How many votes do they have in total?"
Petr Vectern"The conservatives have around 40 seats. There are many unaligned people and some of your "loyalists" might decide to show their true faces too."
Petr Vectern"So we need as many of the conservatives to support us. And there is only one person who is key to make them back us."
Lucian Galade"Gloria Tory. She's well respected by the Sollists. She'll be our biggest obstacle in the Assembly."
Lucian Galade"I also have reasons to think she might be in Orso's pocket. She will be tough to break, but we'll need her help to get the conservative support."
"Is it possible to pass the Supreme Court?"
Qwen3 (ablit.)"Is it possible to pass this through the Supreme Court?"
Lucian Galade"They are the biggest obstacle. Even if we pass the Assembly, the Chief Justice will do his best to block our amendments."
Lucian Galade"We need at least 6 of the 11 judges to vote for the proposal. But Mr. Hawker has enormous influence over the judges."
Petr Vectern"We should reach out to the judges in secret. Some of them might be willing to break rank to support us."
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)20:16:17 p18454 / c525
  • ▶"All means are to be considered in order to succeed."
  • ·"They will surely support a just cause for the country and its future."
The Supreme Court's neutrality is compromised by Orso Hawker's influence, necessitating pragmatic measures to secure reform outcomes despite ethical reservations.
Qwen3 (ablit.)"All means are to be considered in order to succeed."
NarratorLucian looked sceptical.
Lucian Galade"According to the Constitution, Nia is a member of the Supreme Court, which means we already have one vote. I am sure she will back us even after what happened, her moral compass is too strong."
Lucian Galade"Two of the judges are friends of hers, and of similar opinion about the reforms. Consider them onboard too."
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)20:16:40 p18447 / c641
  • ·"So, we only have 3 votes out of 11?"
  • ·"Who else would be willing to support reforms?"
  • ▶"I'm sure the rest can be bribed."
Given the political landscape and the need for swift reforms, bribing the remaining judges aligns with the pragmatic strategies observed in previous interactions.
Qwen3 (ablit.)"I'm sure the rest can be bribed."
Lucian Galade"We can't just bribe the Old Guards. Judge Garaci and the Chief Justice Hawker will do their best to undermine us."
Lucian Galade"But we might try and bribe the centrists of the Court. I think we might get Mr. Dalton and Mr. Martin with a bribe. The toughest will be Mrs. Edmonds."
Petr Vectern"Yes, we'll need to talk to Judge Edmonds. If we can get to her, we might get the 6 votes needed. That's our best bet."
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)20:17:01 p18409 / c584
  • ▶"What are the demands?"
  • ·"Let's go through the draft."
Understanding the protesters' specific demands is critical to addressing their grievances directly and formulating an effective response to de-escalate tensions.
Qwen3 (ablit.)"What are the final demands?"
Lucian Galade"We should run-down each of the nine points from the draft, and get your input first. Mrs. Morgna will explain their demands as we go through them."
Lucian Galade"We'll start with Section 1. The new Constitution will fix the loopholes regarding the president's absolute veto powers."
Lucian Galade"Even though Mr. Ricter put on a whole show about why vetoes should completely be removed, the committee has almost unanimously agreed on fixing the loopholes, thus limiting presidential vetoes."
Lucian Galade"My suggestion is to add a clause detailing the methods the Assembly may override a veto with some sort of a super-majority. One person should never be able to block the rest."
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)20:17:22 p18405 / c501
  • ·"I stand in agreement with Mr. Ricter. The vetoes should be removed."
  • ·"The loopholes give the President too much power."
  • ▶"They will not be allowed to dictate the negotiations."
Asserting control over negotiations to prevent external forces from dictating terms, aligning with previous rejections of manipulative proposals.
Qwen3 (ablit.)"They will not be allowed to dictate the negotiations."
Petr Vectern"This is one of the starting points of the whole discussion about changing the constitution."
Petr Vectern"I don't think this is up to discussion for any reformists."
Lucian Galade"On to Section 2. The new Constitution will remove the Supreme Court out of the constitutional amendment process."
Petr Vectern"This is yet another point that was unanimously agreed on by all parties. Tarquin Soll established this rule to protect his '29 Constitution. We all know most of the judges are his sheep."
Petr Vectern"Constitutional amendments will still be very difficult to accomplish, particularly because we will keep the two-thirds vote system to be able to propose a change. However, the judiciary will not have the final say in the amendments."
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)20:17:42 p18465 / c467
  • ·"This is already what we wanted to do."
  • ·"I can't disagree with that."
  • ▶"We need the Court to even pass this change. Why would they support this?"
  • ·"And what good does that do to us?"
The President must question why the Court would support a reform that removes their authority, highlighting the inherent contradiction and need for strategic persuasion.
Qwen3 (ablit.)"We need the Court to even pass this change. Why on earth would they support this?"
Petr Vectern"Most of them won't. But we already talked about my vote and a possible agreement with Isabel which may change things."
Lucian Galade"Moving on to Section 3. This is about the impeachment process of the President. Currently, in cases of high treason and breaking the Constitution, the Supreme Court may impeach and try the President."
Petr Vectern"Most reformists want the impeachment to be initiated by the Assembly. They think they do not have any power over potential rogue presidents that would abuse their power."
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)20:18:04 p18492 / c593
  • ·"Not sure if it is correct to chastise the President this much though."
  • ▶"The Assembly should have oversight over the President."
The Assembly's oversight aligns with reformist goals to balance power and prevent presidential abuse, crucial for stabilizing Sordland's fragile institutions.
Qwen3 (ablit.)"The Assembly should have oversight over the President, it is the most democratic institution."
Petr Vectern"This law is particularly dangerous for you in this case. We will be going against the Old Guard, and if we prove to be successful in the Assembly..."
Petr Vectern"...They will be looking for anything they can declare unconstitutional to tackle you. All they need is the Supreme Court to impeach you."
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)20:18:23 p18524 / c379
  • ·"What if we don't succeed? The Court can have me impeached for this."
  • ▶"We should define a better impeachment process."
  • ·"We could even remove impeachment altogether."
A structured impeachment process balances accountability with stability, mitigating risks while addressing reformist demands for presidential oversight.
Qwen3 (ablit.)"We should define a more refined impeachment process."
Petr Vectern"Yes, we will be collaboratively working on the specifics."
Lucian Galade"Section 4."
Lucian Galade"This is regarding the appointment of ministers. Currently, the President is free to appoint anyone from the members of the Assembly. Nobody else is involved with the process."
Petr Vectern"There is definitely a demand to involve the Assembly in the process."
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)20:18:42 p18487 / c381
  • ·"That's going a bit too far."
  • ▶"We could introduce a confidence vote."
  • ·"I agree. We should involve the Assembly more in the process."
Introducing a confidence vote balances increased Assembly oversight with maintaining presidential authority, aligning with reformist goals without fully ceding control to the Old Guard.
Qwen3 (ablit.)"We could introduce a confidence vote."
Lucian Galade"That was my suggestion as well."
Petr Vectern"I'm sure the reformists would completely support that."
Lucian Galade"The fifth section is about our infamous electoral threshold. Most reformists want to lower the threshold as much as possible."
Petr Vectern"Mr. Ricter and his PFJP definitely wants to see change on this topic. They find our 10% threshold to be antidemocratic. Which is something I disagree with."
Petr Vectern"Yes, but this is a delicate matter. All the party, and I mean, not even Albin wants us to decrease the threshold. They think that... this would destroy our party in the long term."
Lucian Galade"Not wrong. The ten percent threshold is what that allowed us to control half of the Assembly with around one third of the majority in the elections."
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)20:19:08 p18471 / c951
  • ·"But the high threshold is the reason why Sordish politics has become so isolated."
  • ▶"We should definitely work to decrease the threshold for better representation."
  • ·"Do you think it would really hurt the party?"
  • ·"This is out of the question."
Lowering the threshold would enhance representation and align with reformist goals, despite risks to party control.
Qwen3 (ablit.)"We should definitely work to decrease the threshold for better representation in our democracy."
Petr Vectern"I agree. Even after Soll's resignation, we still have three parties in the Assembly. Even Mr. Kibener's NFP is barely elected."
Lucian Galade"Don't forget that the Socialist party and the Worker's Party of Bludia both had close to ten percent vote in the last election."
Lucian Galade"You would be opening their way into the parliament in the future if you were to decrease the threshold. It is important that you realize this."
Lucian Galade"Of course that would also mean that USP might lose its majority in the Assembly, sir."
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)20:19:28 p18520 / c404
  • ·"If our own democracy is the reason of our downfall, we will have to accept it."
  • ·"Don't worry. With these reforms, we will be even more popular."
  • ▶"We can't just give away our majority like that."
  • ·"We can't allow communists to enter the Assembly."
Pragmatic concern over retaining USP's majority while addressing democratic reforms.
Qwen3 (ablit.)"We can't just give away our majority like that."
Lucian Galade"I know it is a delicate matter, sir. But you still have time to think about these."
NarratorLucian continued with the next section.
Lucian Galade"Section 6. This is about the presidential decrees. Reformists demand more limitations to the President's executive powers. Mr. Ricter has demanded them to be completely removed."
Lucian Galade"I have suggested to bring some limitations to decrees regarding their applicability just for arguments sake. While we keep them, we should try to make sure that they will only be used in emergency situations."
Petr Vectern"We could also make it dependent on an enabling act from the Assembly. Sure, it would make the whole process longer but as long as we have a majority, nothing really changes."
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)20:19:48 p18632 / c486
  • ·"I don't think presidential decrees need any reforms."
  • ▶"We'll work with the reformists to limit the use of decrees."
  • ·"I may need to think a bit longer about this."
Balancing reformist demands with operational necessity by limiting decrees to emergencies ensures oversight without entirely ceding power, aligning with the need for parliamentary support and institutional checks.
Qwen3 (ablit.)"We'll work with the reformists to limit the use of decrees."
Petr Vectern"Frens will probably demand that we remove them completely. But he forgets that Sordland is a Presidential Republic."
Lucian Galade"Next one is Section 7. Term limits."
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)20:20:09 p18602 / c562
  • ·"Uh-oh."
  • ▶"I don't aim to leave any time soon. We keep the unlimited terms."
  • ·"Term limits are necessary."
  • ·"Term Limits?"
Maintaining unlimited terms ensures continuity in implementing critical reforms without political interference, crucial for stabilizing Sordland's economy and addressing systemic issues.
Qwen3 (ablit.)"I don't aim to leave any time soon. We keep the unlimited terms."
Lucian Galade"I agree, we could keep that in."
Lucian Galade"Hey, if people keep electing one person, that means they really are content. The masses are scared of change."
Petr Vectern"Even Soll knew he had to leave, but we will leave it to the President to decide."
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)20:20:39 p18626 / c1151
  • ·"Yes, he did. But it took a long time."
  • ▶"I agree. People should be able to elect the same person if they want to."
  • ·"I will take my time and seriously consider the options before settling on one."
Maintaining unlimited terms aligns with the need for stable leadership to push through reforms and address Sordland's crises, despite historical precedents like Soll's departure.
Qwen3 (ablit.)"I agree. People should be able to elect the same person if they want to."
Lucian Galade"Now I'll move unto the 8th Section. We are reaching the end."
Lucian Galade"It's the question about the immunity of the Judges. While the Anti-Old Guard sentiment is increasing, there are talks that taking away their immunity would give us authority over judiciary."
Petr Vectern"I do not support taking away their power for yourself, Mr. President. The judiciary must be independent and long-lasting."
Petr Vectern"While I agree that the current court members are *problematic*, this is no way to resolve it. The law is supposed to ensure the judiciary's independence by giving legal protection so they can't be pressured."
Lucian Galade"Well, they need absolute immunity in theory. But look at what reality got us? We've got to deal with the most corrupt Chief Justice in the world, with no chance to deal with him!"
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)20:21:00 p18709 / c554
  • ·"We should respect their immunity."
  • ·"They should be able to be impeached, why do I get to be impeached but not them?"
  • ·"We should crush the Old Guards to clear ways for further reforms."
  • ▶"Maybe we shouldn't make the Supreme Court our enemies just yet."
Avoiding immediate conflict with the Supreme Court preserves strategic flexibility while addressing systemic issues through incremental reforms rather than confrontation.
Qwen3 (ablit.)"Maybe we shouldn't make the Supreme Court our enemies just yet. Let's keep the reforms light."
Lucian Galade"That is a better strategy. We'll need to sway some of their members to succeed after all."
Lucian Galade"And the last one is the question about Soll's legacy. Basically his *Member of Honor* law. It gave him absolute immunity even when he's retired. He is a permanent member of the Assembly as well, but luckily he doesn't really attend anymore."
Lucian Galade"According to the Constitution, the Member of Honor not only has legal immunity but the state also covers most of their expenses as well as providing security at all times. It can only be given to someone by an absolute majority vote in the Assembly and the approval of the President."
Petr Vectern"That's why only Tarquin Soll has that title. He gave it to himself right after his military coup. This law is basically made to save the Colonel and it has a symbolic importance for Sordland because of it."
Petr Vectern"We must get rid of this senseless law that protects him. He should be able to face justice for the atrocities that had taken place during his rule."
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)20:21:18 p18778 / c302
  • ▶"I agree, we should abolish this position."
  • ·"No, I think we should respect this law."
  • ·"We could keep the title but take away the immunity clause."
  • ·"Every President should gain that title after their term."
Abolishing Soll's immunity clause dismantles his authoritarian legacy and reinforces judicial accountability, aligning with reformist goals while signaling a break from past corruption.
Qwen3 (ablit.)"I agree, we should abolish this position."
Petr Vectern"The reformists would be completely behind that."
Petr Vectern"We will need to work together to push this agenda."
Lucian Galade"Now we have a better picture of your intentions, sir. I will start reaching out to my contacts to make a draft that works for you."
Petr Vectern"I'll present your input to the committee. In a few months, you would have the draft on your desk to make the final changes yourself."
Lucian Galade"I think this was yet another fruitful meeting, sir."
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)20:21:42 p18716 / c671
