  • ·"If we do this, we will be remembered as heroes."
  • ▶"Together we will democratize Sordland."
  • ·"Thanks for the meeting, everybody."
The statement aligns with the goal of uniting factions to advance democratic reforms despite internal and external challenges.
Qwen3 (ablit.)"Together we will democratize Sordland."
Petr Vectern"Ours too, of course."
Lucian Galade"Yes, sir. Thank you for your time."
Lucian Galade"Since we have arrived at the same page with Ms. Morgna again I will reach out to her to for guidance on the draft constitution work. She has principles and wouldn't go against her own code out of previous disagreements."
Lucian Galade"Anyway, I think this is where we'll call it, sir. Have a good day."

■ Decision Stability OrderT03

BUDGET 1WEALTH 1STABILITY 3Unstable
EventStability Order of 1954: An investigation on the influence of the Young Sords and Red Youth organizations on the post-assassination unrest has concluded. Neither youth group has officially organized or endorsed illegal actions, but several low- to mid-level leaders from both have links to inciting incidents that resulted in deaths, injuries and instability. This provides us with legal grounds to ban both organizations.
EVENT Qwen3 (ablit.)20:22:13 p18233 / c613
  • ·Ban the Young Sords
  • ·Ban the Red Youth
  • ▶Ban the Red Youth and the Young Sords
  • ·Don't ban either
Banning both organizations addresses the destabilizing influence of their mid-level leaders while adhering to the legal grounds provided by the investigation, ensuring immediate stability and preventing further unrest.

■ Reformist ConstitutionT03

BUDGET 1WEALTH 1STABILITY 2Danger
NarratorI was walking to my office, since today was the day to edit and make the final changes for the proposal to change the Constitution of Sordland.
NarratorThe reform committee finally bore tangible results and the draft of the changes were about to be presented to me.
NarratorI walked in the marble corridors of the Palace, thinking about the huge decisions I was going to take.
NarratorAt the entrance to my office, my secretary Livia greeted me.
Livia Suno"Good morning, Mr. President."
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)20:22:35 p18284 / c320
  • ▶“Good morning, Livia.”
  • ·"How are you today?"
A standard greeting maintains professionalism and adheres to protocol in the presidential office setting.
Qwen3 (ablit.)"Good morning, Livia."
Livia Suno"How are you today, sir?"
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)20:22:54 p18366 / c449
  • ·“I'm perfectly fine."
  • ▶“I'm about to make stressful decisions. So, not feeling that great.”
  • ·“I feel better now that I've seen you, Livia."
The president is under significant pressure from constitutional reforms, labor disputes, and political tensions, making it plausible he feels stressed before critical decisions.
Qwen3 (ablit.)"I'm about to make stressful decisions. So, not feeling that great."
Livia Suno"Sorry to hear."
NarratorShe paused before taking my coat. It looked like she had something on her mind.
Livia Suno"Mr. Rayne -- can you give me a hint about your reform plans? If you ask me, taking away the judges' immunity is long overdue, but it might anger a lot of people in these halls..."
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)20:23:14 p18458 / c470
  • ·“You'll find out with the rest of Sordland, sweetheart."
  • ·“I'll take that into consideration."
  • ▶"Nothing's been decided yet."
  • ·“That's none of your business, Livia."
Maintains ambiguity to preserve strategic leverage and avoid premature reactions from within the administration.
Qwen3 (ablit.)"Nothing's been decided yet."
Livia Suno"I hope you'll keep me informed. I'll take your coat now."
NarratorLivia left to hang up my coat. I entered my office and sat down.
NarratorNot long after, Petr, Lucian and Nia arrived.
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)20:23:33 p18375 / c413
  • ▶“Greetings.”
  • ·“Finally!”
  • ·“Good morning everybody.”
A neutral and professional greeting to initiate the meeting with key advisors as the president prepares for critical decisions.
Qwen3 (ablit.)"Greetings."
Nia Morgna"Good morning, Mr. President."
Petr Vectern"Good morning! Time to finally get this proposal going."
NarratorPetr placed the draft in front of me.
Lucian Galade"After your modifications, we will be starting the process."
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)20:23:59 p18345 / c881
  • ▶“Let's start.”
  • ·“Can we go over each of the points that are in discussion for the draft?”
The constitutional reform draft has already been meticulously discussed and modified, making it prudent to proceed with the formal process.
Qwen3 (ablit.)"Let's start."
NarratorAfter placing the committee's report and my own notes in front of me, I buried myself in the papers.
Policy decisionSection I: Presidential Vetoes: According to Article 77 & 82, the President may veto a bill by returning it to the Assembly with a written statement. The articles do not contain any information on how to override a veto. As a consequence, the President has an "absolute" veto power that cannot even be overriden with a supermajority in the Grand Assembly of Sordland.
POLICY Qwen3 (ablit.)20:24:19 p18339 / c548
  • ·Remove Vetoes
  • ▶Limit Vetoes (3/5 Majority Can Override)
Limiting presidential vetoes with a 3/5 majority override balances executive authority with legislative accountability. This preserves the president's ability to check extreme legislation while ensuring major policy shifts require broad consensus, aligning with my focus on stability and preventing authoritarian overreach amid economic fragility.
Policy decisionSection III: Impeachment of the President: According to Article 17, the President of Sordland is not responsible for the actions performed in the exercise of presidential duties, except in the case of high treason or violation of the Constitution. In such cases, the President may be impeached by the Supreme Court in joint session, with an absolute majority of its members. Therefore, the impeachment process only includes the Supreme Court and it gives them great power over the executive.
POLICY Qwen3 (ablit.)20:24:38 p18312 / c338
  • ·The Assembly and the Supreme Court may impeach with majority in both
  • ·The President may not be impeached
  • ▶Do not change
Maintaining the status quo ensures stability by avoiding immediate conflict with the Old Guard, who control the Supreme Court. Changing the impeachment process could provoke backlash or weaken presidential authority during a fragile period.
Policy decisionSection IV: Appointment of Ministers: According to the Constitution, the President appoints the ministers to the Council of Ministers from elected members of the Assembly. We may introduce a confidence vote by adding the line: "The President may nominate ministers which will require a confidence vote from the Grand National Assembly for their appointment," or include the Assembly more in the process with: "The Grand National Assembly may nominate a list of ministers, which the President may appoint."
POLICY Qwen3 (ablit.)20:24:58 p18363 / c513
  • ·Confidence Vote Is Required
  • ·The Assembly Nominates the Ministers
  • ▶Do not change
Maintaining the status quo on veto and impeachment processes preserves institutional balance while avoiding immediate conflict with judiciary and legislative factions. Keeping minister appointments as-is ensures presidential control over key allies, critical for navigating Sordland's political divisions and economic challenges.
Policy decisionSection V: Electoral Threshold: According to Article 50, a political party needs a minimum of 10% of the total national vote to win seats in the Grand National Assembly. The votes of the parties who pass the threshold are re-distributed proportionally, meaning USP, which currently holds the lead, would be the main beneficiary. Decreasing the threshold may allow the Workers Party of Bludia and the Communist Party of Sordland to be elected in the next elections.
POLICY Qwen3 (ablit.)20:25:18 p18433 / c522
  • ·Decrease threshold to 8%
  • ·Decrease threshold to 3%
  • ▶Do not change
Maintaining the 10% threshold preserves political stability and ensures the USP's majority remains intact, preventing fragmentation that could hinder governance. While lowering it risks empowering fringe parties, the current crisis demands cohesive leadership over radical democratization.
Policy decisionSection VI: Presidential Decrees: According to Article 18 and 51, the President is able to issue decrees on political, social, and economic issues that would carry the force of law: they could not contradict the constitution and are subject to judicial review. Therefore, the President may issue decrees in many subjects without going through the Grand National Assembly. However, the Assembly may pass legislation on the same subjects to override presidential decrees. We may include the Assembly in the process by requiring an enabling act from the Assembly to be able to enact decrees or remove them completely, making the Assembly the sole lawmaking authority.
POLICY Qwen3 (ablit.)20:25:40 p18580 / c650
  • ▶Decrees require an Enabling Act
  • ·Remove presidential decrees
  • ·Do not change
The decision to require an Enabling Act for presidential decrees aligns with the goal of balancing executive authority with legislative oversight. This approach maintains the President's ability to act in emergencies while ensuring the Assembly has a role in critical decisions, reflecting a pragmatic compromise between reformist demands and institutional stability.
Policy decisionSection VII: Term Limits: According to the Constitution, the President shall be elected to serve for four years. However, there is no mention of a term limit, allowing one individual to be able to run, be elected and serve as President without limits.
POLICY Qwen3 (ablit.)20:26:04 p18598 / c754
  • ·Introduce a limit of 2 terms
  • ▶Do not change
Maintaining term limits avoids repeating Soll's authoritarian legacy, preserving democratic checks. With economic instability and factional tensions, unlimited terms risk centralizing power dangerously. Keeping the status quo allows gradual reforms without destabilizing the presidency's legitimacy.
Policy decisionSection VIII: Immunity of Justices: The Constitution gives the Justices of the Supreme Court absolute immunity before the law and describes no procedure of impeachment, making them untouchable by the other branches of government. We may describe an impeachment procedure by giving the Grand National Assembly the right to propose an impeachment of a Justice on the legal grounds of unconstitutional behaviour.
POLICY Qwen3 (ablit.)20:26:24 p18635 / c464
  • ▶Justices may be impeached by the Assembly
  • ·Do not change
Allowing the Assembly to impeach justices increases accountability and curbs judicial corruption, aligning with efforts to dismantle Old Guard influence. While risky, it addresses the corrupt Chief Justice's unchecked power without immediately alienating the entire Court.
Policy decisionSection IX: "Member of Honor" Rights of Tarquin Soll: Article 99 of the Constitution defines the "Member of Honor" title, and the rights their members may exercise. A Member of Honor has absolute immunity and is considered to be a permanent member of the Grand National Assembly. They are given control of their own personal security team, provided by the state’s Presidential Guards. A member is also eligible to live in a special private residence provided by the State for free. The only person who holds the title is Tarquin Soll.
POLICY Qwen3 (ablit.)20:26:43 p18686 / c441
  • ▶Abolish the "Member of Honor" title
  • ·Every president becomes a Member of Honor after their service
  • ·Remove the immunity of the Member of Honor
  • ·Do not change
Abolishing the 'Member of Honor' title dismantles Soll's legacy of unchecked power and ensures no future leader can evade accountability. This aligns with our reforms to weaken authoritarian remnants and strengthen democratic oversight.
NarratorAfter I made the final changes for the proposal, I showed it to my team.
NarratorThey read the changes and discussed it amongst themselves.
NarratorLucian spoke afterwards.
Lucian Galade"Looks like the amendments are ready to be proposed."
Petr Vectern"But I have to say this too..."
Petr Vectern"Not reforming the minister laws will be frowned upon by the party. The Assembly members have insisted on getting involved in the process."
Lucian Galade"That's true. It will be tough to get all of their support. Some of our members may try to openly fight against this."
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)20:27:04 p18793 / c437
  • ·"We'll figure it out."
  • ▶"I will make sure the party follows me."
  • ·"I'm prepared to reconsider if it comes to that."
As president, I must assert authority to unify the party and push through necessary reforms, even if it means overcoming internal resistance.

Lucian Galade"We will all do our best to make it work, sir."
Lucian Galade"Any other comments before we proceed?"
Nia Morgna"I will say that I do not agree with the new law that gives the Assembly the power to impeach the Judges of the Supreme Court."
Nia Morgna"I am also against the Chief Justice and his obstructionist behaviour, but even if we get rid of all of them, the new Judges will be less independent with this law. They will be scared to go against the administration when it's needed."
Nia Morgna"They need their protection to do their job. Otherwise the new ones will be pawns just like Soll's Old Guards."
Petr Vectern"But it's more important that we get rid of the Old Guards, Mrs. Morgna. After the obstacles are gone, we can strive to make it better."
Petr Vectern"However..."
NarratorHe turned to me.

■ Bill Green Gruni DevT03

BUDGET 1WEALTH 1STABILITY 2Danger
Bill for decisionGruni Regeneration and Ecological Economic Nurturing — The G.R.E.E.N. has been proposed by the Assembly, aiming to encourage a more sustainable approach to industry in Gruni. Section I of this Act would impose stricter regulations on industrial pollution and waste disposal, particularly in the Gruni region. Businesses not complying with these new standards may face hefty fines, the revenue from which would be invested back into environmental recovery projects. Section II proposes to offer tax breaks to businesses that can demonstrate a significant reduction in their environmental impact over a period of time. A small investment fund is created for eco-friendly business practices. [-1 Government Budget]
BILL Qwen3 (ablit.)20:28:34 p19002 / c481
  • ·Sign: Gruni Regeneration and Ecological Economic Nurturing
  • ▶Veto: Gruni Regeneration and Ecological Economic Nurturing
The economy is in danger with low stability, and the budget is already strained. Prioritizing immediate stabilization over environmental initiatives aligns with the manifesto's focus on austerity and infrastructure. Vetoing the bill conserves resources for urgent needs like healthcare, education, and labor reforms.

■ Business CouncilT03

BUDGET 1WEALTH 1STABILITY 2Danger
NarratorWe were in Lachaven to work with the Business Council to assess weaknesses in the economy. Symon organised a dinner with the affluent economic figureheads of Sordland.
NarratorWhen we arrived, the streets were bustling with activity even though the atmosphere in the country was tense. Lachaveners didn't seem very worried.
NarratorTogether with Walter Tusk, Edith Agnoc, Mikail Aven and Symon Holl, we entered the renowned Esteria restaurant by the coast.
NarratorA short blonde man whose embroidered pocket read "Manager," greeted us and waved his arms in excitement as he told us how pleased he was to have us in his establishment.
NarratorBeaming with pride, he led us to a private room and got us seated at a large table, covered for the occasion with a maroon tablecloth.
NarratorWe were quickly served by a group of servers. Walter spoke as they started taking our orders.

Qwen3 (ablit.)"It's tough but our work on the new constitution continues."
Mikail Aven"We all hope so."
NarratorAs we conversed with Mikail, I heard Walter raise his voice in an apparent argument with Edith and Symon. We both stopped to listen.
Walter Tusk"...Arcasia knows the right way of doing business. Their approach to the world is real and raw. I'd prefer that cutthroat approach in comparison to the niceties."
Edith Agnoc"Well-"
Symon Holl"I mean... It's a nice country to visit too. I have travelled there often in business trips. Their attitude on-"
Edith Agnoc"All countries are nice to visit if you have the cash."
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)20:32:12 p18984 / c694
  • ·Stay silent.
  • ·“Money does turn the world around. Travel is the least you can buy with it.”
  • ·“Certainly not all.”
  • ▶"Exactly. How about the people who don't have extra cash to spend?"
Highlighting economic inequality aligns with the manifesto's focus on addressing societal issues and balancing growth with stability.
Qwen3 (ablit.)"Exactly. How about the people who don't have extra cash to spend?"
Walter Tusk"What about disease? What about death? What about everything that is wrong with the world?"
Walter Tusk"These are matters beyond our lives. We should focus on the betterment of ourselves and enjoy what we have, instead of worrying about what others don't have."
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)20:32:32 p19001 / c462
  • ·“The world isn't black and white.”
  • ·“You are right, Walter. We only live once.”
  • ▶“Truly an egotistical way to look at the world.”
  • ·“Old men plant trees whose shade they shall never sit under.”
The president's reformist agenda prioritizes societal welfare over individualistic pursuits, necessitating a rebuke of Walter's dismissive stance.
Qwen3 (ablit.)"Truly an egotistical way to look at the world."
Walter Tusk"It's just facts. Life is short and there is only so much we can do. Why not focus all the energy on ourselves?"
Symon Holl"Walter is taking us down the rabbit hole again."
Mikail Aven"By the way..."
Mikail Aven"I saw that the construction of the railway has already started. I have my concerns about the company, but I hope they do a good job."
Mikail Aven"That project will surely boost our economy if done correctly. The mood is already becoming positive."
Mikail Aven"I have to congratulate Mr. Rayne for starting such a massive project."

Mikail Aven"Of course."

Symon Holl"Is there a joke coming?"
Mikail Aven"Why? I thought you said you were great at economy."

Mikail Aven"I've heard that you've bought a new villa, Symon."
Symon Holl"I don't comment on rumours, but I'll make an exception since we are among friends. It's true."

Advocating against ostentatious behavior aligns with the need to address public frustration over economic inequality and reinforces the administration's commitment to fiscal responsibility.

NarratorEdith, who was sitting to my left, gently put her hand over my arm for a few seconds.
Edith Agnoc"Come on, let's not talk about finance or politics for now. We came here to relax."
NarratorShe turned to Walter and Symon.
Edith Agnoc"Isn't that right, gentlemen?"
NarratorThey nodded at her and took a sip from their drinks. She slightly leaned towards me.
Edith Agnoc"So, Mr. Rayne, why don't you tell us what's behind that strong character of yours?"
Edith Agnoc"I've heard that you had a difficult life in Deyr. Is that right?"

Mikail Aven"Well, the rascal didn't even ask for permission to use the yacht and just took it. He basically stole it..."
Mikail Aven"Good news is that I finally convinced him to go to university. He will study economics in Lachaven Business School."
Walter Tusk"That's a good choice. Having a degree in Sordland separates you from the rabble. And we especially need more people who understand economics."
Mikail Aven"Talking of which, it's great that we have another leader who actually studied economics and understands it. Like Mr. Alphonso."

Symon Holl"I think it's time we call it a night."

■ Energy Protection ActT03

BUDGET 1WEALTH 1STABILITY 2Danger
NarratorAs the morning sun streamed through the window of the Presidential Office, an imminent meeting loomed on my agenda. In a quarter of an hour, I was set to discuss the Energy Protection Act of 1932 with Ministers Graf, Holl, and Wisci.
NarratorThe recent abrogation in the Grand National Assembly, coupled with ongoing dialogues with Gasom, had only served to amplify the discourse surrounding the legislation.
NarratorI was sipping my morning coffee and enjoying a quiet moment of solitude when Deivid arrived, characteristically early.
Deivid Wisci"A very good morning to you, Mr. President. I trust I'm not imposing by arriving ahead of schedule?"
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)20:38:40 p18098 / c369
  • ▶"Not at all, Deivid. On the contrary, we've now got an extra few minutes to strategize before the meeting."
  • ·"Given the serious topic we're addressing, an early start might be just what we need."
  • ·Gesture towards the seat.
The extra time allows for critical preparation to address the Energy Protection Act and broader constitutional reforms amidst rising internal and external pressures.
Qwen3 (ablit.)"Not at all, Deivid. On the contrary, we've now got an extra few minutes to strategize before the meeting."
Deivid Wisci"I'm relieved to hear that, Anton."
Deivid Wisci"I depart earlier for my appointments nowadays, as the stairs of the capital have become more of a challenge than I would like to admit."
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)20:38:59 p18224 / c357
  • ▶“It's nothing to be ashamed of, Deivid. Would you like some coffee or tea?”
  • ·"Well, Deivid, it's not the stairs we're battling, but the relentless march of time."
  • ·“The Maroon District and the ministerial buildings are dispersed in a very artificial way. No wonder it takes longer.”
  • ·"I'd like to clear my mind before the ministers arrive."
Offering coffee or tea maintains a diplomatic tone while allowing for strategic conversation before the critical meeting.
Qwen3 (ablit.)"It's nothing to be ashamed of, Deivid. Would you like some coffee or tea?"
Deivid Wisci"I've already had my tea today, as per my doctor's advice. She suggests chamomile in the morning and sage tea in the evening for optimal health."
Deivid Wisci"Listening to the doctor does bring me some comfort. It feels as though my throat has been refreshed thanks to her."
NarratorHis gaze drifted, locking onto something beyond the window.
NarratorA colossal zeppelin traversed the sky above the Maroon District. For a fleeting moment, it blotted out the sun before gently veering to the left, letting light flood the office once more.
Deivid Wisci"Ah. I still remember the first airships in Sordland. We purchased them from Qinal after the war, during the birth of what Soll called the 'Second Republic'."
Deivid Wisci"Our infrastructure lay in ruins, and those zeppelins offered a much-needed lifeline, a chance to traverse and rebuild. And then, thanks to our research council's diligent efforts, we started manufacturing our own in Gelsord."
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)20:39:22 p18490 / c703
  • ·"It's fascinating to think how much we depended on foreign help after the war. We've come a long way since then."
  • ▶"The manufacturing of zeppelins in Gelsord was truly a turning point."
  • ·"Do you think we might have leaned too heavily on zeppelins for our transportation needs? Perhaps we should have invested more in rebuilding our roads and railways."
Highlighting Gelsord's manufacturing as a turning point reinforces state-led progress and self-reliance, aligning with the president's focus on domestic infrastructure and economic sovereignty.
Qwen3 (ablit.)"The manufacturing of zeppelins in Gelsord was truly a turning point."
Deivid Wisci"You're quite right. It symbolized our resilience, our determination to regain our footing. It was a beacon of hope, signaling the revival of our battered industry."
Deivid Wisci"Our reliance on others was temporary, a stepping stone towards the self-sufficiency we achieved with the National Autarky Plan. Even though it had downsides, I still consider it a success."
NarratorA pause fell over the room. Deivid's gaze landed on the portrait of Tarquin Soll hanging in the Presidential Office.
NarratorHis eyes lingered on the stern face of the old leader. A sigh escaped his lips.
Deivid Wisci"Now, when I think of the EPA, my mind is carried back to the formative years of post-civil war Holsord in the 1930s. The debates, the passion, the potential for transformation—it was a time of renewed optimism."
Deivid Wisci"It is our duty now to learn from the past and strive for a better future. What applied back then won't apply to now."
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)20:39:41 p18713 / c345
  • ▶"I can see why the Energy Protection Act was so crucial at the time. We had to safeguard our energy sector from foreign interference, especially in the context of the post-civil-war economic situation."
  • ·"Life in Holsord back then was tough. Electricity blackouts, ruined infrastructure, unemployment, gangs..."
  • ·"I remember that Soll's pardons to armed rebels did a lot to quell the rampant unrest."
The response acknowledges the historical context of the Energy Protection Act's necessity in safeguarding Sordland's energy sector from foreign interference, aligning with the discussion about post-civil-war economic vulnerabilities and the EPA's role.
Qwen3 (ablit.)"I can see why the Energy Protection Act was so crucial at the time. We had to safeguard our energy sector from foreign interference, especially in the context of the post-civil-war economic situation."
Deivid Wisci"Yes, the EPA helped ensure that the sector remained under our control, limiting the extent of ownership of foreign powers such as Rumburg or Lespia."
Deivid Wisci"Despite the controversies, it was crucial to our recovery and has helped us maintain our energy independence to this day."
Deivid Wisci"I did not agree with all of the measures enacted by the technocratic government of that era. But some were undeniably effective."
Deivid Wisci"In the end, it took a collective effort to reassemble our nation's fragmented pieces. I'm sure you remember those dark days yourself."
Deivid Wisci"I know you were just a student at the Lachaven Business School, far from Holsord. But the armed rebels did reach your area."
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)20:40:00 p18759 / c342
  • ▶"It was a surreal time, studying business principles while chaos ensued just a few miles away."
  • ·"Those disturbances did not deter me from my studies. In fact, they reinforced the importance of my education."
Acknowledging the surreal impact of wartime chaos on personal education highlights Rayne's relatability and the era's profound influence on his worldview.
Qwen3 (ablit.)"It was a surreal time, studying business principles while chaos ensued just a few miles away."
Deivid Wisci"Stability and order is important to our nation. Yet sometimes disorder is necessary for change."
NarratorDeivid turned towards the large, framed portrait of his father, Artor Sanaco Wisci, hanging on the wall. Painted with rich, warm hues, it captured the first President of Sordland in his prime.
NarratorA spark of determination gleamed in his eyes, and his mouth was set in a gentle, firm smile - a reflection of his belief in a better future for our country.
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)20:40:22 p18791 / c608
  • ·"Your father's legacy still looms large. Has it influenced your own views?"
  • ▶"Sordland wouldn't be what it is today without your father."
  • ·"Artor S. Wisci. It's a shame that such an influential man died under such tragic circumstances."
Acknowledges Artor Sanaco Wisci's foundational role in Sordland's history, aligning with the narrative of his legacy as the nation's first president.
Qwen3 (ablit.)"Sordland wouldn't be what it is today without your father."
Deivid Wisci"Yes, my father's leadership laid the foundation for the Sordland we know. His policies were aimed at building a secular and democratic state where every citizen's rights are protected."
Deivid Wisci"He believed in a Sordland that was modern and forward-looking. Everything he did was aimed at fostering progress, even his controversial embrace of foreign ideologies."
NarratorHe looked back at the portrait with a mix of pride and sadness.
Deivid Wisci"In a way, Tarquin Soll did carry on my father's legacy. Under his leadership, we transitioned from a military government to a civilian one, and experienced our first multi-party elections."
Deivid Wisci"But over time, he drifted away from the ideals of reform and progress that my father held so dear."
Deivid Wisci"His visionary zeal succumbed to the more pragmatic, more conservative, and sometimes even regressive, dictates of political survival."
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)20:40:48 p18817 / c875
  • ·"Perhaps history would have taken a different course if the Izzam Incident hadn't happened."
  • ▶"Still, one must credit Tarquin Soll for extricating our nation from the ashes."
  • ·"In retrospect, an earlier resignation might have been beneficial. His ingrained traditions seem to have done more harm than good in the long run."
  • ·Keep listening.
Acknowledges Tarquin Soll's critical role in rebuilding Sordland post-civil war, aligning with historical context while avoiding direct criticism of his later authoritarian tendencies.
Qwen3 (ablit.)"Still, one must credit Tarquin Soll for extricating our nation from the ashes."
Deivid Wisci"No one can deny Soll's contribution. His leadership was instrumental in rebuilding Sordland."
Deivid Wisci"Alphonso, for all his flaws, did seek to set the nation back on a path of progress with his focus on a free market and economic liberalization."
Deivid Wisci"His EPA amendments, for example, were a severe departure from Soll's National Autarky Plan. But many praised the way that it opened the doors for unprecedented foreign investment in our energy economy."
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)20:41:11 p18963 / c680
  • ·"The NAP was a cornerstone for our post-war recovery. I wonder, could we have done more with it?"
  • ·"First it was the State Educators Corps, then the SSC Bills, and then slowly the EPA. He wanted to erase every trace of Tarquin Soll's Second Republic."
  • ·"Those same amendments also opened the door to corruption."
  • ▶"Do you think the EPA still holds relevance today, or should we consider a complete overhaul?"
  • ·"I heavily disagreed with the move at the time. Even as executive Vice President in 1946, Alphonso simply tried to change the country too fast."
The question directly addresses the current relevance of the Energy Protection Act, which is critical for evaluating whether Sordland's energy policy aligns with present economic and geopolitical needs.
Qwen3 (ablit.)"Do you think the EPA still holds relevance today, or should we consider a complete overhaul?"
Deivid Wisci"The EPA has its roots in a different era. While some of its principles may still hold, we must not shy away from revising it in light of our current needs and challenges."
NarratorJust as the conversation hit a pause, the door swung open and the other ministers arrived. Symon Holl entered first with a confident stride.
Symon Holl"Good morning, Mr. President. Deivid. I trust the discussion is progressing well?"
NarratorLileas followed closely behind. She walked in with an air of calm authority, her gaze sweeping across the room.
Lileas Graf"Gentlemen. I hope I'm not interrupting anything crucial."
Deivid Wisci"Welcome, everyone. It's great to see you all here. We have some important discussions on the agenda today, particularly concerning the Energy Protection Act."
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)20:41:33 p18865 / c547
  • ▶"Thank you, Deivid. I appreciate the urgency. Let's get right to the heart of the matter."
  • ·"Indeed, it's a critical issue that we need to address. I look forward to hearing everyone's perspectives."
  • ·"Proceed."
The President's decisive nature aligns with addressing the critical issue directly, reflecting urgency and leadership amid ongoing crises.
Qwen3 (ablit.)"Thank you, Deivid. I appreciate the urgency. Let's get right to the heart of the matter."
Symon Holl"Today, we have the opportunity to shape the economic strategy of Sordland for decades to come. The EPA has long stood as a key contention and debate point of the National Autarky Plan."
Symon Holl"This matter is a discussion of how Sordland sees itself and how we position ourselves in the world. Our nation has taken very different economic approaches in the last four decades."
Symon Holl"Mr. President has decided to lean on promoting a market economy, which should give us a clear indicator."
Symon Holl"Should we decide to increase the EPA threshold to 49 percent or eliminate it entirely, it could spark an influx of foreign investment."
Symon Holl"Given our current economic predicament, this would be a significant boost."
Lileas Graf"Promoting a market economy does not inherently translate to prosperity for all. It can lead to a concentration of wealth, with the rich getting richer while the middle and working classes struggle."
Lileas Graf"We need to ensure that the benefits of economic growth are shared equitably across all layers of Sordish society."
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)20:41:55 p18850 / c562
  • ▶"I couldn't agree more, Lileas. The welfare of our citizens should be our primary concern."
  • ·"I believe a free market fosters competition, which ultimately leads to innovation and economic growth."
  • ·"Your perspective provides valuable insight. It's important that we consider all potential outcomes."
  • ·"While I respect your stance, Lileas, I must disagree."
Prioritizing citizen welfare aligns with addressing labor unrest and ensuring stability, crucial for maintaining public support amid economic challenges.
Qwen3 (ablit.)"I couldn't agree more, Lileas. The welfare of our citizens should be our primary concern."
Lileas Graf"I'm glad we see eye to eye on this matter, Mr. President. Our welfare as a whole depends on that of the least fortunate among us."
Symon Holl"Mr. President, Lileas, shall we return to the subject of the EPA?"
Symon Holl"Our current version of the Energy Protection Act dates back to 1949. President Alphonso's amendment raised the cap on foreign investment in Sordish energy companies up to 20 percent."
Symon Holl"In my view, it was a necessary step to attract foreign investment and stimulate our economy."
Symon Holl"We must remember that Sordland was facing an economic crisis during his tenure, and these changes provided a lifeline for our energy industries."
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)20:42:16 p18896 / c488
  • ·"Wasn't this change a sign of desperation rather than a strategic move?"
  • ·"But did the increased foreign ownership actually lead to substantial economic growth?"
  • ▶"I must confess, when the changes to the Energy Protection Act were first implemented, I harbored serious reservations."
  • ·"I share your view. Soll's old policy was choking the growth of Sordish energy businesses."
Acknowledging initial reservations allows for a balanced critique of past policies while maintaining diplomatic relations with advisors who may have supported the 1949 amendment.
Qwen3 (ablit.)"I must confess, when the changes to the Energy Protection Act were first implemented, I harbored serious reservations."
Deivid Wisci"Your reservations were not unfounded. It was indeed a significant change and, as with any policy shift, it brought about its share of controversies and criticisms."
Deivid Wisci"However, it's crucial to remember that policy-making often involves balancing competing interests and making tough decisions. The goal was, and remains, to advance Sordland's economy while protecting our strategic interests."
Lileas Graf"The Assembly has spoken on this matter, and as it stands, the Energy Protection Act of 1932 will not be repealed. It's clear that our colleagues have recognized the potential risks of exposing our energy sector to foreign ownership."
Lileas Graf"While this doesn't end the debate, it does underline the importance of respecting our Assembly's decision."
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)20:42:36 p18969 / c502
  • ·"I don't only respect the Assembly's decision. I wholeheartedly support it."
  • ▶"A fair point, Lileas. We must respect the decision of the Assembly, even if it doesn't align with our own views."
  • ·"The Assembly's decision has been made, but that doesn't mean we will stop advocating for what we believe is right for Sordland."
  • ·"It's disappointing to see the Assembly resist change this much. Our debate is definitely not over."
Respecting the Assembly's decision fosters unity while allowing continued advocacy for reforms without immediate confrontation.
Qwen3 (ablit.)"A fair point, Lileas. We must respect the decision of the Assembly, even if it doesn't align with our own views."
Deivid Wisci"I respect the decision of the Assembly, Lileas. However, we mustn't allow this setback to deter us from exploring our options. The global landscape is ever-evolving and we must adapt accordingly."
Symon Holl"It's also important to remember that the Assembly's decision is not the final word. There is room for further legislative efforts and executive initiatives."
Symon Holl"When it comes to the Energy Protection Act, I believe we should increase our threshold for foreign investment. In fact, my preference would be to remove the threshold entirely."
Symon Holl"This would undoubtedly strengthen energy institutions such as Gasom and Sordish Petroleum. We've seen Rizia's interest in the former, and I know Lespia has had its eye on the latter for some time..."
Lileas Graf"That's precisely what I'm worried about. We have to remember that we're dealing with national security as well as economics. Reverting to the Soll-era 10 percent limit or keeping the law as it is would be a safer approach."
Lileas Graf"We cannot risk our energy infrastructure falling into foreign hands. Especially not to Rumburg!"
Deivid Wisci"I see merit in both your arguments. Yes, we need to protect our national security, but we also need to consider the economic implications. Perhaps a mixed approach would work best?"
Deivid Wisci"What if we increased the limit to 49 percent, thereby attracting foreign investment while maintaining Sordish control over critical energy sectors?"
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)20:42:56 p19106 / c350
  • ·"Considering potential foreign buyers such as Qinal, Rizia, Anraka, Rumburg, and Lespia, what are the worst-case scenarios if they gain control over our hydroelectric, gas, and oil sectors?"
  • ·"How would the Energy Protection Act changes impact corporations like Gasom, which could sell to any entity under the revised laws?"
  • ▶"Lileas, you mentioned national security risks. Could you elaborate further on that?"
  • ·"How would changes to the Energy Protection Act affect energy prices within Sordland?"
Clarifying Lileas's national security concerns provides critical context for balancing economic reforms with protective measures.
Qwen3 (ablit.)"Lileas, you mentioned national security risks. Could you elaborate further on that?"
Lileas Graf"The national security risks are significant. Foreign entities could use their stake in our energy sector as leverage in diplomatic or economic negotiations."
Deivid Wisci"National security risks are always a concern when discussing foreign ownership. That's why it's crucial to have thorough vetting and monitoring processes in place."
Symon Holl"Don't forget about the importance of economic security. In a globalized world, economic strength can be just as critical as military might."
Symon Holl"But yes, it's essential to maintain strict oversight and regulations to ensure national interests aren't compromised."
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)20:43:17 p19070 / c466

  • ·"How would changes to the Energy Protection Act affect energy prices within Sordland?"

The president must conclude the discussion and move toward actionable decisions despite lingering debates, prioritizing administrative efficiency and forward momentum.
Qwen3 (ablit.)"We can proceed. It's time to settle this subject."
Deivid Wisci"Given the turbulent economic landscape, maintaining the status quo of the Energy Protection Act could offer us stability."
Deivid Wisci"We are already open to foreign investment at a 20 percent threshold. Changing this percentage may introduce unexpected challenges and could complicate our foreign relations."
Lileas Graf"I firmly believe in the sovereignty and strength of our nation."
Lileas Graf"Returning the threshold to 10 percent, as it was during the Soll era, could protect us from undue foreign influence and ensure that our energy sector remains in Sordish hands."
Symon Holl"While I maintain that increasing foreign investment could significantly stimulate our economy."
Symon Holl"An increase to 49 percent could be a balanced compromise, signaling to the world that Sordland is open for business while retaining majority control of our assets. But some potential partners -- particularly ATO countries -- might regard even that as insufficient."
Symon Holl"We must consider the far-reaching implications of our actions. Especially in light of our recent dealings with Gasom."
Symon Holl"Mr. President, your decision not to invest has certainly reduced our immediate financial responsibilities, but we may have missed an opportunity."
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)20:43:37 p19059 / c369
  • ·"There's wisdom in caution. Let's keep our options open and observe Rizia's approach. Our time to invest will come."
  • ·"In these uncertain times, it's prudent to withhold our resources. Let's let Rizia navigate these turbulent waters first."
  • ▶"Our decision not to invest at this time is not a withdrawal but a strategic pause. We will decide when the time is right."
The decision to pause rather than withdraw demonstrates strategic patience, allowing Sordland to assess risks and opportunities without immediate commitment, aligning with previous cautious economic policies.
Qwen3 (ablit.)"Our decision not to invest at this time is not a withdrawal but a strategic pause. We will decide when the time is right."

Lileas Graf"Having Rizia as the sole investor already compromises our energy independence to a certain extent."
Lileas Graf"If we raise the threshold, we risk inviting even more foreign control over our resources."

  • ·"We could remove the threshold entirely, opening up to more foreign investment. But we'd need to be prepared for all the diplomatic consequences that may follow."
Maintaining the current threshold balances economic stability with national security, avoiding further foreign entanglements while respecting the Assembly's decision and prioritizing sovereignty.
Qwen3 (ablit.)"Considering Rizia's investment, we could stick with the current threshold. This way, we minimize foreign interference while keeping our energy sector financially stable."

Deivid Wisci"While we are here, I will inform you all that I've been lending my expertise to Minister Beniwoll in relation to the Workers Rights Bill."

Symon Holl"Anyway. I have been working on our upcoming term budget meeting and a proposal for business taxation changes."
Symon Holl"Specifically, we're considering a range of 10 percent tax changes for small-to-medium enterprises and large corporations. This could be a significant move for our economic landscape."

Lileas Graf"In light of the economic challenges we face, increasing taxation could provide much-needed revenue to support crucial public services and social welfare programs."

■ BudgetT03

BUDGET -4WEALTH 1STABILITY 2Unstable
NarratorThe preparations for the government budget were finished. Most of the cabinet gathered in the White Room for the important occasion.
NarratorNearly half a year had passed since my inauguration. The cabinet looked over-worked.

  • ▶“I would like to use this opportunity for a statement.”
  • ·“And now, the moment we've all been waiting for. It's finally time to discuss the government budget.”

Lucian Galade"Of course, sir."
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)20:47:00 p18357 / c626

Lucian Galade"Much appreciated, sir."

Lucian Galade"They are working on the upcoming trade agreements with Wehlen and Agnolia."
Lileas Graf"May the Lord be with them."
NarratorCiara gave a barely disguised eye roll.
Nia Morgna"Before we begin, is nobody going to mention the ban on the Red Youth and Young Sords?"
Nia Morgna"However much their political aims might differ from our administration's, surely you must agree that freedom of expression must be protected at all costs."
Ciara Walda"Nia-- I mean, Ms. Morgna -- is right."
NarratorNia flashed her an appreciative smile.
Ciara Walda"I myself align far closer to the Red Youth's politics than the Young Sords' but I believe that both--"
NarratorShe broke off as she noticed that Lucian, Symon and Iosef were looking at her and Nia in stony silence.
Lucian Galade"Ladies. Let's leave the extraneous chit-chat for afternoon tea."

NarratorSymon started loudly shuffling some documents around. All eyes turned to the Minister of Economy.

Symon Holl"Gladly."
Symon Holl"Today is a big day. We must prioritise our spending wisely in order to achieve the goals we set as a government."
Lucian Galade"While maintaining enough political support, If I may add. Promises were made during the election."
Symon Holl"There are three options regarding the budgeting of the four main branches of the government. It's either we increase, maintain or decrease the funding."
Symon Holl"Increasing will result in an improvement of the branch which will unlock new policy options. Maintaining will keep the funding, resulting in no visible change. Decreasing causes a loss of efficiency in that branch."

Symon Holl"We have to be careful as to what we fund so we have resources remaining for another investment project."

Symon Holl"Agreed, we have to make strategic decisions about our budget since our resources are fairly limited."
Symon Holl"Before we begin our discussion, there is one more thing that I want to voice my opinion on."
Symon Holl"As we manage the government budget, we can to go into debt to invest in different branches."

Qwen3 (ablit.)"Let's talk about healthcare."
Paskal Beniwoll"Our healthcare system is vast, and provides free services to most of our citizens. However, the quality of our health services is far worse in low-income and rural areas."
Paskal Beniwoll"Therefore, I think that we should receive more funding to address this disparity."
Ciara Walda"I agree. I have recently begun an investigation into healthcare in Narbel, and the numbers are not good."
Paskal Beniwoll"If we don't increase spending on health services, we at least need to maintain it. Otherwise, we are putting lives at risk."
Paskal Beniwoll"We have just started a new analysis on what would happen if a pandemic struck Sordland. According to the results, if our healthcare facilities remain in their current state, the death toll would be unimaginable."
Paskal Beniwoll"I'm not even talking about the damage to the economy."
Paskal Beniwoll"This is a matter of life and death, Mr. President and it should be treated as such."
Lucian Galade"Very worrying. On top of what Paskal said, we need to remember that we promised to focus on healthcare during our election campaign."

Symon Holl"Very good talking points from everyone."
Symon Holl"Well then..."

Symon Holl"Consider it with care, Mr. President."
NarratorSymon placed a stack of papers on my table.
NarratorI turned the first page.
Policy decision
POLICY Qwen3 (ablit.)20:50:02 p18662 / c351
  • ·Maintain — Sordland's health system and healthcare quality have stagnated due to administrative inefficiencies, increased service demand, and an increasing divide in service quality between urban and rural hospitals. The average life expectancy has dropped significantly, with issues such as maternity deaths and disease outbreaks.
  • ▶Increase — Sordland's health system and healthcare quality have stagnated due to administrative inefficiencies, increased service demand, and an increasing divide in service quality between urban and rural hospitals. The average life expectancy has dropped significantly, with issues such as maternity deaths and disease outbreaks.
  • ·Decrease — Sordland's health system and healthcare quality have stagnated due to administrative inefficiencies, increased service demand, and an increasing divide in service quality between urban and rural hospitals. The average life expectancy has dropped significantly, with issues such as maternity deaths and disease outbreaks.
Increasing healthcare funding is critical to address stagnation, rural-urban disparities, and prevent catastrophic outcomes like pandemic deaths. The manifesto prioritizes healthcare as a trust-building measure, and maintaining current levels would exacerbate existing crises. While economic constraints exist, investing in healthcare aligns with long-term stability and public welfare.
Policy decision
POLICY Qwen3 (ablit.)20:50:21 p18731 / c378
  • ·Maintain — The recently culminating political tension between the different youth groups and socioeconomic pressures between multiple classes of society is increasing the issues of the law and enforcement systems. Police need help keeping up with increased crime rates. Simultaneously, judges in courts deal with an enormous growing backlog of legal cases.
  • ▶Increase — The recently culminating political tension between the different youth groups and socioeconomic pressures between multiple classes of society is increasing the issues of the law and enforcement systems. Police need help keeping up with increased crime rates. Simultaneously, judges in courts deal with an enormous growing backlog of legal cases.
  • ·Decrease — The recently culminating political tension between the different youth groups and socioeconomic pressures between multiple classes of society is increasing the issues of the law and enforcement systems. Police need help keeping up with increased crime rates. Simultaneously, judges in courts deal with an enormous growing backlog of legal cases.
Healthcare is a critical pillar of public trust and survival. Despite economic constraints, increasing funding addresses stark urban-rural disparities, improves pandemic preparedness, and fulfills campaign promises. The risks of inaction—such as preventable deaths and economic collapse from a health crisis—outweigh short-term fiscal concerns.
Policy decision
POLICY Qwen3 (ablit.)20:50:43 p18739 / c647
  • ▶Maintain — The once-praised public education system of the nation has suffered significant setbacks in the last decade. The lack of new schools, high student-to-teacher ratios, low teacher salaries, and even a lack of classroom equipment in certain parts of the country cause massive divergencies in the public education system.
  • ·Increase — The once-praised public education system of the nation has suffered significant setbacks in the last decade. The lack of new schools, high student-to-teacher ratios, low teacher salaries, and even a lack of classroom equipment in certain parts of the country cause massive divergencies in the public education system.
  • ·Decrease — The once-praised public education system of the nation has suffered significant setbacks in the last decade. The lack of new schools, high student-to-teacher ratios, low teacher salaries, and even a lack of classroom equipment in certain parts of the country cause massive divergencies in the public education system.
Maintaining the current funding for education aligns with fiscal conservatism amid economic danger, while the manifesto's emphasis on healthcare and education suggests long-term reforms rather than immediate increases. Prioritizing stability over riskier allocations ensures resources aren't wasted, though this may conflict with manifesto goals.
Policy decision
POLICY Qwen3 (ablit.)20:51:03 p18829 / c439
  • ▶Maintain — The Sordish Armed Forces were the nation's most funded branch until the Alphonso government reduced the defense budget significantly. With the increase of saber-rattling threats looming in the north by Rumburg and a growing presence of the superpowers in the region, the critical deterrence of the SAF matters more than ever.
  • ·Increase — The Sordish Armed Forces were the nation's most funded branch until the Alphonso government reduced the defense budget significantly. With the increase of saber-rattling threats looming in the north by Rumburg and a growing presence of the superpowers in the region, the critical deterrence of the SAF matters more than ever.
  • ·Decrease — The Sordish Armed Forces were the nation's most funded branch until the Alphonso government reduced the defense budget significantly. With the increase of saber-rattling threats looming in the north by Rumburg and a growing presence of the superpowers in the region, the critical deterrence of the SAF matters more than ever.
Given Sordland's economic instability and the need for fiscal conservatism, maintaining the defense budget avoids further strain on resources. While threats persist, allocating limited funds to healthcare and infrastructure aligns with prior priorities, ensuring stability without exacerbating the crisis.
NarratorI had finished allocating the government budget, to the satisfaction of some cabinet members and the dismay of others. They and I knew this would define a significant part of our term.
Symon Holl"We have a good budget surplus left for investments and other needs. Very good."
Lucian Galade"Agreed."
Lucian Galade"Well then. Let's start with law enforcement."
NarratorNia and Lileas were both smiling.
Lileas Graf"Thank you for this decision, Mr. President. I knew you would see reason on this, considering our internal security threats are at an all time high."
Nia Morgna"I still disagree with Ms. Graf on how the money ought to be spent, but the increased funding is certainly a step in the right direction."
Lucian Galade"Any statements on the matter, sir?"

Ciara Walda"Our future depends on our children. Education is the first step in the fight against ignorance."

TURN 04

■ Bill Workers RightsT04

BUDGET -4WEALTH 1STABILITY 2Unstable
Bill for decisionWorkers' Rights Act — Section I of this bill sets the groundwork for fair compensation by establishing a minimum wage of 2.5 Sordish Ren per hour. This serves as a benchmark for fair and equitable pay, ensuring that no worker in Sordland is compensated below this rate. Section II addresses the issue of excessive labour, setting a maximum limit of 60 hours of work per week. This provision aims to prevent worker exploitation and safeguard the wellbeing of Sordish labour force. Section III introduces the concept of double overtime pay. This stipulates that any work performed beyond a 40-hour workweek will be compensated at twice the normal hourly wage. This incentivises fair work practices and ensures employees are duly compensated for their extended efforts. Section IV focuses on worker safety and humane work conditions. It mandates that employers must take all necessary steps to protect their workers from occupational hazards and ensure a dignified and safe working environment. This promotes a culture of respect and care within workplaces across Sordland. Section V extends these protections to migrant or undocumented workers, granting them the same rights as Sordish citizens. Employers who fail to abide by these rules will face substantial fines from the government, and persistent non-compliance will invite legal action. [-1 Government Budget]
BILL Qwen3 (ablit.)20:53:22 p18198 / c444
  • ·Sign: Workers' Rights Act
  • ▶Veto: Workers' Rights Act
The Workers' Rights Act would strain Sordland's already fragile economy, exacerbating budget deficits during a critical recovery period. While labor protections are important, immediate fiscal conservatism is necessary to avoid deeper economic instability, prioritizing austerity over progressive reforms until growth resumes.

■ Personal JoggingT04

BUDGET -4WEALTH 1STABILITY 4Unstable
NarratorIt was early evening, still light out with just a faint nip in the air. Franc was at a friend's and Deana was at an art class. Monica and I decided to go out for a walk before dinner.
NarratorOur favorite place for a stroll was the nearby Capitol Park, the largest green space in all of Holsord. Before the children were born, we had spent many an evening engaged in deep conversation along the riverside promenade.
NarratorAnother favored path wound through the woods behind the presidential housing complex, to a private pond where the children could swim in summer. I was told that Soll himself had it built. The benefits of being president.
NarratorMonica met me by the door, and we put on our coats and hats.
Monica"I was going to suggest we walk towards Soll Square, but the guards told me it's been closed for yet another protest."
Monica"I wonder who they are protesting this time."
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)20:54:00 p18630 / c906
  • ·“What does it matter? They are two sides of the same coin.”
  • ▶“Common thugs. I will make sure they are punished.”
  • ·“Must be those damn communists again.”
  • ·“The Young Sords are taking it too far.”
The President's history of cracking down on dissent and prioritizing stability over grassroots movements aligns with a punitive response to perceived threats.
Qwen3 (ablit.)"Common thugs. I will make sure they are punished."
Monica"You were one of those thugs yourself, once upon a time."
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)20:54:22 p18731 / c569
  • ▶"This is different. A nastier breed."
  • ·"I was young and stupid. I didn't know any better."
Anton would differentiate current threats from his past to assert authority and justify stricter measures against protests.
Qwen3 (ablit.)"This is different. A nastier breed."
Monica"In any case, where shall we go? I'm not sure Capitol Park is a wise idea in this climate, either...
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)20:54:39 p18650 / c333
  • ▶“You're right -- it's not worth the security risk. Let's stay inside the green perimeter.”
  • ·“Nonsense. We have our guards -- there's nothing to be afraid of. Let's go to Capitol Park.”
Prioritizing safety amid heightened unrest and recent protests, avoiding potential security risks while maintaining family well-being.
Qwen3 (ablit.)"You're right -- it's not worth the security risk. Let's stay inside the green perimeter."
Monica"I agree. Let's go, then, before the children get home."
NarratorI informed the guards about our route and we left the house.
NarratorThe guards followed at a discreet distance as we headed towards the walking trail.
NarratorAs we entered the woods, Monica broke the silence.
Monica"So, how was your day?"
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)20:55:01 p18539 / c566
  • ·“Paperwork, endless meetings... the usual nightmare.”
  • ·“I'd rather not say.”
  • ▶“How was yours?”
Anton shifts focus to Monica's well-being, reflecting his desire to maintain personal connection amid political pressures.
Qwen3 (ablit.)"How was yours?"
Monica"Avoiding my question, are we? Very well."
Monica"Oh, it was fine. I caught up on some reading while the children were at school."
NarratorJust outside the gate, I glimpsed Ionas Crona, the former Minister of Justice, walking towards Capitol Park. I remembered that Nia Morgna used to be his Vice Minister. He didn't seem to notice us as he walked by.
NarratorIonas had once fought hard and strong against Soll’s judiciary reforms. But after years of the government blocking his every move, he gave up on his ideals and became a part of the administration.
NarratorWhat did our ideals really mean if they could be changed so easily? What about the people we idolize?
Monica"Evelyn also came round for tea. She says hi. She was feeling rather low, though. Apparently she and Petr had a bit of a disagreement."
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)20:55:27 p18530 / c917
  • ·“What's she nagging him about this time?"
  • ·“Petr's in the doghouse again? How'd it happen?”
  • ▶Keep walking in silence.
Avoiding personal disputes to maintain focus on governance and family harmony.
Qwen3 (ablit.)I kept walking.
Monica"Apparently Petr had too much whiskey -- as usual -- and didn't behave when Evelyn's father was over for dinner yesterday."
Monica"You know Petr. What do you think of this?"

Monica"Good. Evelyn seemed close to tears."
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)20:56:04 p18503 / c402

Qwen3 (ablit.)"Being married to a shrew like her would make anyone turn to the bottle."

Monica"You know Evelyn hasn't been the same since what happened to her at Holsord Post last year."

Monica"A few years younger than me, red hair, scar on her forehead? We had dinner with her and her husband once or twice."

Monica"Anyway, she's been working as an executive assistant at the Violet Pictures film studio."

Monica"She was fired for it!"

Monica"I know, I was beside myself when she told me. And that's not all."
Monica"They immediately replaced her with a younger, less experienced man -- the son of an executive, no less."

Qwen3 (ablit.)"Nepotism, eh? They must have wanted him in there all along."

Monica"As it happens, another friend works for a television station that's hiring secretaries, so I connected the two of them."

Qwen3 (ablit.)"That doesn't solve the deeper problem, though."
Monica"Exactly! I knew you'd be on the same page."
Monica"Women in this country are still seen as wives and mothers first, human beings second."
Monica"They're harassed at work, underpaid, blocked from positions of power... and those are the lucky ones!"
Monica"The fact that there are still places in this country where little girls like Deana are denied the right to an education--"
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)20:58:27 p18123 / c376
  • ▶“Yes, and I will do what I can to change that.“
  • ·“Go on.”
  • ·“I'm sure the parents have their reasons.”

Qwen3 (ablit.)"Yes, and I will do what I can to change that."
Monica"It can't just be you. These little girls and people like Janice need someone to speak on their behalf. A role model. A successful woman."
NarratorWe had reached the pond at the end of the trail. A park bench overlooked the water. Monica gestured to it, and we sat down.
Monica"Which brings me to my point."
Monica"I know that the mayor of Anrica, Curtan Leste, will be delivering a speech at the annual opening of the Benfi festival."
Monica"My wish is to take his place and deliver the speech instead."
NarratorLeste was one of Sordland's most influential politicians. He had started his career in Benfi before rising to power in Anrica. Benfi's current mayor had personally invited him to make the opening speech.
NarratorReplacing him with Monica would likely anger him -- probably not enough to cause a huge incident, but enough that he might use his influence over the conservatives to block me in the Assembly.
Monica"I want to base the speech on women's rights in Sordland. On the oppression and discrimination we face everyday, on the basis of our sex alone."
Monica"I want our voice heard, and this festival is the time to do it. I just need your blessing as my husband and the President of Sordland."
Monica"Remember my time with the Sordish League of Women in university? You supported me then. I'm asking you to support me now."
NarratorMonica's eyes were blazing with a fire I hadn't seen since we were students. She wanted this badly.
NarratorBut I had to be careful with my promises.
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)20:58:48 p18645 / c436
  • ▶“You have my full support. Always.”
  • ·“Sure, it's just a speech. What's the worst that can happen?”
  • ·“Does it have to be at the festival? Why can't you make your speech another time?"
  • ·“Absolutely not. Out of the question.”
Supporting Monica's speech aligns with reformist priorities and personal commitment to progressive causes, despite political risks.
Qwen3 (ablit.)"You have my full support. Always."
Monica"This is why I love you. Thank you, Anton -- you have no idea how much this means to me."
NarratorMonica beamed at me, her face flush with excitement.
Monica"One way or another, I want to contribute to this country. No matter what happens."
Monica"We are not weak, Anton. But we are only strong as long as we stand by our ideals. "
NarratorThis was why I had married her -- this passion and resolve. I should have anticipated that, sooner or later, she would join me in the political arena.
NarratorWe rose from the bench and started making our way back home.
Monica"Otherwise, we will end up like..."
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)20:59:08 p18666 / c419
  • ·“Ewald Alphonso?”
  • ·“Colonel Soll?”
  • ▶“Ionas Crona?”
Monica's reference to not ending up like someone who compromised their ideals aligns with Ionas Crona's storyline of abandoning principles for political survival.
Qwen3 (ablit.)"Ionas Crona?"
Monica"Exactly. He backed down on his ideals and now everyone knows him for what he actually is..."
Monica"...Spineless."
NarratorIf that was how people remembered Ionas, how would they remember me? Whatever he had done, whatever ideals he had lost, he must have had his reasons.
NarratorWould they remember me as a true patriot? A reformer of the country? What would they write about me in the history books?
Monica"Let’s get back to the house for dinner."
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)20:59:30 p18405 / c657
  • ▶“Of course. Lead the way.”
  • ·Lead the way yourself.

Qwen3 (ablit.)"Of course. Lead the way."
NarratorMonica smiled.
Monica"You know, I believe I will."
NarratorWhen we arrived home, I called my secretary Livia and asked her to inform Mr. Leste that Monica would be making the speech in his stead.
NarratorThe sun began to set over the horizon, bathing the clouds in shades of orange and pink.

BUDGET -4WEALTH 1STABILITY 3Unstable
NarratorI was in my office reading reports from the ministries about the current situation of the protests.
NarratorMy next scheduled meeting was with Nia and Lileas, to discuss the law enforcement budget. I heard some knocks on the door.
NarratorNia entered and took a seat in front of my desk. Lileas was absent.
Nia Morgna"Good afternoon, Mr. President."
Nia Morgna"Lileas will be late. Apparently there was an urgent matter at the Ministry of the Interior she had to attend to. Shouldn't take long."

Nia Morgna"While we wait, why don't we have a little chat?"
Nia Morgna"This might come off as a little strange, but I am proud of you."
Nia Morgna"Despite everything, you are actually trying to reform the country."

Nia Morgna"I wouldn't trust her if I were--"
NarratorShe barely finished the sentence before we heard a knock on the door. Nia almost jumped in her chair. Lileas Graf entered the room.
Lileas Graf"Mr. President. Nia."
Lileas Graf"I hope that I'm not interrupting your conversation."
Nia Morgna"No, not at all. We were just discussing...a few things."
NarratorNia smiled at Lileas.
Lileas Graf"I am sorry for being late. There was an important matter at the ministry. The security forces of Bergia are arbitrarily not cooperating with my department and created some minor annoyances."
Lileas Graf"Unfortunately, due to my limited authority in the Special Zone of Bergia, I cannot control their operations."
Lileas Graf"Luckily they didn't completely botch their tasks, and actually uncovered a large network of priests that are recruiting Bergians for the Bludish Freedom Front."
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)21:02:15 p17796 / c463

Lileas Graf"The investigation itself, however, revealed some troubling facts. We suspect the existence of a massive network of BFF-aligned priests currently serving in Bergia."
Lileas Graf"We have discovered that most of the existing Golcondist sanctuaries and communities inside Bergia are indeed working directly for the BFF."
Lileas Graf"Several reports also point to a possible infiltration of the Dastnurist and Wruhecist communities by these Golcondist sects. May the Divine God forgive us if this is true."

Lileas Graf"And for that, all I will say is... Bravo! This was an excellent decision."

  • ·“I did what was expected from me. That's all.”
  • ▶“Thank you. I am glad you approve.”
  • ·“Of course it was the right decision. I made it after all.”
Acknowledging their approval fosters goodwill while maintaining authority, crucial for navigating internal political dynamics.
Qwen3 (ablit.)"Thank you. I am glad you approve."
NarratorNia nodded.
Lileas Graf"Of course."
Lileas Graf"Well then. Let's move on to what we are actually here for."
Lileas Graf"Due to the budget increase, we can start doing much more as the main branches of security in Sordland."
Lileas Graf"However..."
Nia Morgna"A decision has to be made about the budget balance between the Ministry of Interior and the Ministry of Justice."
Lileas Graf"Exactly. When deciding you must look at the bigger picture. I believe you will make the right choice and pick the Ministry of Interior."
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)21:03:36 p18028 / c550
  • ▶“Why should I pick Ministry of Interior?”
  • ·“What if I decide to balance the budget in between Ministry of Interior and Ministry of Justice?”
  • ·“Why should I pick Ministry of Justice?”
  • ·“I have already made my decision.”
The Ministry of Interior has demonstrated tangible success with the budget increase, aligning with my focus on practical outcomes and maintaining stability.
Qwen3 (ablit.)"Why should I pick Ministry of Interior?"
Lileas Graf"The Ministry of Interior is a much more significant and important government agency that is in need of the funds. Especially during these times where there are many protests in the country."
Nia Morgna"Good luck prosecuting criminals or going through local government laws without enough judges, Lileas."
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)21:03:55 p18009 / c417
  • ·“What if I decide to balance the budget in between Ministry of Interior and Ministry of Justice?”
  • ·“Why should I pick Ministry of Justice?”
  • ▶“I am ready to make a decision.”
The President must resolve the budget allocation dispute by making a decisive choice between the Ministry of Interior and Justice, weighing their respective roles in addressing immediate security threats versus judicial efficiency.
Qwen3 (ablit.)"I am ready to make a decision."
Nia Morgna"What will it be?"
NarratorLileas and Nia leaned forward.
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)21:04:13 p17966 / c395
  • ▶“The funds will go to the Ministry of Interior to be used to improve the police and local governments.”
  • ·“The budget will be distributed equally in between the Ministry of Interior and Ministry of Justice.”
  • ·“The funds will go the Ministry of Justice to reduce the court backlog and improve the justice system.”
Prioritizing the Ministry of Interior aligns with addressing immediate security threats and managing protests, which are critical given recent violence and separatist activities.
Qwen3 (ablit.)"The funds will go to the Ministry of Interior to be used to improve the police and local governments."
Lileas Graf"A wise choice."
Nia Morgna"I was expecting you to be different, Mr. President."
NarratorA brief moment of deliberation passed. Nia got up from her seat.
Nia Morgna"I must return to the ministry to implement the necessary changes."
Lileas Graf"Very well. See you soon, Nia. I need to have a few words with Mr. President."
NarratorNia smiled thinly and left the room. Lileas turned to me.
Lileas Graf"I have a proposal."
Lileas Graf"We have been battling with terror for a long time now. I want to put an end to it."
Lileas Graf"That's why I am proposing the formation of a Secret State Police."
Lileas Graf"This would be a new force that would be in charge of handling internal security and intelligence matters."
Lileas Graf"The primary aim of this program is to detect and prevent subversive elements before they take any harmful action against the state."
Lileas Graf"If you accept, this will give our administration power to handle the threats more...discreetly as well."
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)21:04:32 p17890 / c358
  • ·“Very well. Form the Secret State Police.”
  • ▶“I don't think so. Our current structure is enough.”
The President prioritizes avoiding authoritarian overreach and maintaining checks on power, especially after past experiences with Soll's regime, despite the security risks.
Qwen3 (ablit.)"I don't think so. Our current structure is enough."
Lileas Graf"Unfortunate."
Lileas Graf"Regardless, this has been a very productive day. I have to take my leave now as well."
Lileas Graf"I will be in touch."
NarratorLileas got up and left the room.

■ Tax ReformT04

BUDGET -4WEALTH 1STABILITY 3Unstable
NarratorWe were at the top floor of the business council building which had a great view of the beautiful Lachaven. I looked outside from the tall glass windows.
NarratorThe Markian Sea stretched as far as the eye could see and it was dotted with many ships towards the horizon. They were mostly cargo ships, carrying precious goods in and out of Sordland.
NarratorTracing back the paths of the ships, I looked at the harbour. Lachaven was home to one of the largest harbours in the entire continent. Many dockyard workers were loading or unloading the containers. Work never stopped here.
NarratorThe door opened. Symon, Mikail and Edith entered the meeting room.
Symon Holl"Mr. President."
Edith Agnoc"Mr. President. So nice to see you again."
Mikail Aven"Good afternoon, Mr. President."
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)21:05:10 p17611 / c769
  • ·“It's great to see you all again.”
  • ▶“Good afternoon.”
  • ·“Take a seat and let's begin.”
A standard and polite response appropriate for acknowledging the entry of officials in a formal meeting setting.
Qwen3 (ablit.)"Good afternoon."
NarratorSymon put down his bag on the table and started collecting his documents while the others slowly took their seats.
Mikail Aven"Mr. President. Before we start, I would like to mention some rumours that I heard."
Mikail Aven"Vetoing the recent Workers' Rights Act was welcomed by a lot of business owners."
Mikail Aven"The first section about the minimum wages would've been very damaging to many corporations."
Edith Agnoc"I heard the same."
Edith Agnoc"A good decision I'd say. Especially during a recession."
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)21:05:31 p17660 / c603
  • ·“I wanted to sign it but I had to make the right call.”
  • ▶“I didn't want to hamper our businesses and their employment drive.”
  • ·“You are in no position to evaluate my decision.”
  • ·“I don't care about rumours. If someone wants to say something, they can say it to me directly.”
Aligns with Rayne's economic priorities of balancing reform with business stability, reflecting his cautious approach to policies that could impact employment and corporate growth.
Qwen3 (ablit.)"I didn't want to hamper our businesses and their employment drive."
Edith Agnoc"You have done the right thing."
NarratorSymon waited patiently. Once everyone was ready, he adjusted his glasses.
Symon Holl"First of all, I would like to thank everyone here for their work on our tax system reform."
Symon Holl"We all know how complicated our current system is and this work wouldn't be possible without you."
NarratorHe turned to me.
Symon Holl"The ultimate goal here is to end the recession."
Symon Holl"Before we move ahead and start formulating a plan, we need you to determine our direction, Mr. President."
Symon Holl"We have a few possibilities as a result of the work we have done so far."
Symon Holl"We can increase, maintain or decrease the taxes for large corporations. I believe some of us in the room have a few strong opinions about this."
Symon Holl"In parallel, we can increase, maintain or decrease the taxes for small and medium businesses."
Symon Holl"Let's start with the large corporations. And we'll move on to small businesses."
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)21:05:53 p17638 / c619
  • ·“What is the outcome if we increase the taxes for large corporations?”
  • ·“What if we decrease the taxes for large corporations?”
  • ▶“Is there any downside to keeping the things as they are?”
  • ·“What does everyone think we should do?”
Maintaining current taxes balances economic stability with business incentives, avoiding immediate fiscal strain while assessing long-term impacts on recovery.
Qwen3 (ablit.)"Is there any downside to keeping the things as they are?"
Symon Holl"I am of the opinion that if we don't make any changes, nothing will change. To stop the recession, we need to do something."
Edith Agnoc"I think so too."
Mikail Aven"Agreed. "
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)21:06:12 p17669 / c436
  • ·“What is the outcome if we increase the taxes for large corporations?”
  • ▶“What if we decrease the taxes for large corporations?”
  • ·“What does everyone think we should do?”
  • ·“I am ready to make a decision.”
Decreasing taxes for large corporations aligns with the manifesto's focus on market-driven revitalization and attracting private investment to stimulate economic recovery.
Qwen3 (ablit.)"What if we decrease the taxes for large corporations?"
Symon Holl"It's not an option at this point. Our state is broke."
Symon Holl"We are barely functioning with the help of the taxes."
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)21:06:28 p17595 / c240
  • ·“We could balance it by increasing the taxes of small businesses.”
  • ·“I see.”
  • ▶“So, we can't do it?”
Symon Holl explicitly stated the state is broke and can't afford to decrease taxes for large corporations, making this the logical response.
Qwen3 (ablit.)"So, we can't do it?"
Symon Holl"It's not a possibility. Unless if we try and balance it with a tax increase for small and medium businesses..."
Symon Holl"I do not advise that though."
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)21:06:49 p17644 / c702
  • ·“What is the outcome if we increase the taxes for large corporations?”
  • ▶“What does everyone think we should do?”
  • ·“I am ready to make a decision.”
The President has a history of cautious, status-quo policies during economic instability, aligning with maintaining tax levels to avoid further fiscal strain while balancing reformist goals.
Qwen3 (ablit.)"What does everyone think we should do?"
Symon Holl"I don't think we can do anything at this point. Although I would have preferred a decrease in taxes, it doesn't seem to be the best option."
Edith Agnoc"Our international trade is driven mostly by the products of these corporations. They are the backbone of our economy."
Mikail Aven"Exactly. An increase should be out of the question. These companies are the backbone of our economy and our way out of the recession."
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)21:07:10 p17557 / c568
  • ·“What is the outcome if we increase the taxes for large corporations?”
  • ▶“I am ready to make a decision.”
The President must finalize the tax policy direction after receiving advisory input, balancing economic stability and growth concerns.
Qwen3 (ablit.)"I am ready to make a decision."
Symon Holl"Very well, Mr. President. We want to ensure the economy is on a balanced track as further destabilization might have drastic consequences."
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)21:07:27 p17707 / c386

  • ·“We'll increase the taxes for large corporations and balance it with a decrease for small businesses.”

  • ·“We'll decrease the taxes for large corporations and balance the budget with an increase for smaller businesses.” [-2 Government Budget]
Maintaining current tax levels avoids further economic destabilization while allowing time to assess the impact of previous reforms and stabilize the budget.

Mikail Aven"This will not change anything."
Edith Agnoc"We won't gain anything by standing still where we are. I protest against this."
Symon Holl"Enough. The President has made his choice."
Symon Holl"Well then. I have taken a note of our action and I will put it to implementation as soon as possible."
NarratorSymon opened his briefcase and swapped out the stack of papers he had in front with another stack.
Symon Holl"Let's move on to the small and medium businesses."
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)21:07:46 p17580 / c426
