  • ·“What if we decrease the taxes for small and medium businesses?”
  • ▶“Is there any downside to keeping the things as they are?”
Maintaining the status quo avoids immediate economic disruption and aligns with cautious fiscal policy amid ongoing instability.
Qwen3 (ablit.)"Is there any downside to keeping the things as they are?"
Symon Holl"Sordland needs change to get out of recession. But the current state of the economy is in shambles. There is not much change we can do about taxes."
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)21:08:02 p17586 / c294
  • ·“What is the outcome if we increase the taxes for small and medium businesses?”
  • ·“What if we decrease the taxes for small and medium businesses?”
  • ▶“I am ready to make a decision.”

Symon Holl"Very well."

Qwen3 (ablit.)"Decrease the taxes for small and medium businesses."
Symon Holl"Very well. I have taken another note. We will look into how to go about implementing these changes."
Symon Holl"Well then... Thank you everyone. That will be all for today."
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)21:08:40 p17561 / c422

  • ·“Enjoy Lachaven.”

NarratorThey left the meeting room. I decided to stay a bit longer.
NarratorI went by the windows again and kept watching the sea in silence for some more time.

■ SSCRailway IssueT04

BUDGET -7WEALTH 1STABILITY 3Unstable
NarratorThe L-1 Railway construction created the perfect opportunity to visit the green technological nerve centre of Sordland. Dozens of electrical engineers from the city were part of the electrification upgrade of the rail track.
NarratorThe main purpose of our visit was to solve construction issues that began to endanger the deadline.
NarratorThe Gelsord Research Council let us use their facilities, but the rectors weren't interested in our affairs.
NarratorSymon looked a little more stressed than usual. Gus was also here to find solutions. He eyed me for a while.
NarratorThere was also a message of support from the mayor of Anrica, Curtan Leste, who was grateful for the L-1 investments.
Symon Holl"We scheduled the project to finish in late 1955 but I have concerns about the construction due to progress being slower than expected."
Gus Manger"I expected some issue even before we started this. This corporation is full of fossils that don't know how to solve problems reactively."
Gus Manger"The SSC reported that they have been slowed down due to several areas on the way to Gelsord having a potential for landslides that could endanger the workers. My argument is that they should have known about it."
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)21:09:06 p17896 / c415
  • ·“Do we have a case of project mismanagement?”
  • ·“How can we speed up the construction to get back on track?”
  • ·“You were right. The administration won't work with the SSC again.”
  • ▶“I need to hear solutions not excuses or reasons.”
Demanding solutions instead of excuses ensures actionable progress amid construction delays, aligning with the need to address infrastructure challenges decisively.
Qwen3 (ablit.)"I need to hear solutions not excuses or reasons."
Gus Manger"Excuse my bluntness, sir. The management of SSC is hard to communicate with because they have the know-it-all attitude."
Symon Holl"It would be possible to demand a change in leadership."
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)21:09:27 p17973 / c593
  • ·“The SSC might need more resources to speed things up. That deadline must be met.”
  • ▶“We can't let mismanagement delay the project. It is unacceptable.”
  • ·“The incompetent managers should be fired. This is a disgrace.”
The President prioritizes accountability and efficiency, rejecting complacency in project management to ensure critical infrastructure goals are met without unnecessary delays.
Qwen3 (ablit.)"We can't let mismanagement delay the project. It is unacceptable."
Symon Holl"Couldn't agree more. This shouldn't keep going on any longer."
Gus Manger"You are right. My advice would be to warn them. There is a chance they might wake up to the situation."
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)21:09:45 p18033 / c396
  • ·“Giving them a serious warning could be the solution. A slap on the wrist can do wonders.”
  • ·“Officially warning them is a possibility but isn't tangible, we need action.”
  • ▶“The state owns the SSC. They can't retaliate and bite off the hand that feeds them.”
The state's ownership of SSC provides direct leverage to enforce changes without retaliation, making decisive action feasible.
Qwen3 (ablit.)"The state owns the SSC. They can't retaliate and bite off the hand that feeds them."
Symon Holl"I don't expect them to bite back but maybe they can further slow the construction by protesting."
NarratorSymon took a deep breath and continued.
Symon Holl"One way or another we have to solve this issue. This can lead to real complications down the line otherwise."
Symon Holl"We can either give the construction managers more money, warn them or replace them."
Gus Manger"I say we replace them with more competent managers. It should help resolve the resources being handled inefficiently."
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)21:10:05 p18060 / c473
  • ·“Give more money to the construction managers so they can hire more workers and buy machinery to hit the deadline.” [-1 Government Budget]
  • ·“Warn the construction managers. Send a formal warning from the Ministry of Economy and the Presidency.”
  • ▶“Replace the construction managers with more competent people to ensure the deadline is hit.”
Replacing incompetent managers directly addresses mismanagement, ensures accountability, and prioritizes project completion over bureaucratic inertia, aligning with the need for decisive action to meet deadlines and maintain control over critical infrastructure.
Qwen3 (ablit.)"Replace the construction managers with more competent people to ensure the deadline is hit."
Symon Holl"It's good to see that failure isn't rewarded and punished. We will find more competent people through our networks."
Gus Manger"I'll make some calls. Got good contacts that can be added to the potential candidates."
Symon Holl"That should be it. Good that we have figured out a way to move the process forward."
NarratorThe meeting ended. The three of us toured the Advanced Research and Development divisions in Gelsord.
NarratorThe day-long visit ended with a dinner involving the most important people in the city. Many of them were scientists.
NarratorLater, we made our way back to Holsord.

■ School VisitT04

BUDGET -7WEALTH 1STABILITY 3Unstable
NarratorAs part of the planned program, we arrived in Leiren to visit a middle school.
NarratorThe prestigious Toren Hill School was situated close to the suburbs just outside of the city centre. The building was flanked by two hills, each beautifully dotted with shrubs and flowers.
NarratorLucian, Ciara and I got out of our cars and met at the entrance. Our presence had not gone unnoticed. Apart from our guards, there were at least ten reporters following us around.
Lucian Galade"That must be the principal. Right this way, sir."
NarratorThe principal was a man of short stature with a bushy moustache and a pair of glasses. He approached us quickly, but was stopped by the security guards. They started patting him down and searching him.
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)21:10:57 p17996 / c491
  • ·“That won't be necessary. Let him through.”
  • ▶Wait for them to finish.
Prioritizing security protocol to ensure no threats are present before engaging with the school's principal, maintaining control over the situation.
Qwen3 (ablit.)I waited for them to finish searching the principal. One of the guards turned to me and nodded.
Principal"Mr. President. Such an honor to meet with you."
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)21:11:14 p17967 / c356
  • ▶Shake his hand and smile for the cameras.
  • ·Shake his hand.
Shaking hands and smiling for cameras reinforces a positive public image and aligns with promoting education and community engagement during a school visit.
Qwen3 (ablit.)We shook hands and I turned around to smile for the cameras. Reporters instantly snapped a few photos.
Principal"Welcome to our humble school."
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)21:11:35 p17953 / c608
  • ·“Sorry about the security guards. It's a necessity. Standard procedure.”
  • ▶“It's good to be here.”
  • ·“Let's begin.”
A positive acknowledgment of the visit aligns with maintaining diplomatic relations and emphasizing the importance of educational institutions.
Qwen3 (ablit.)"It's good to be here."
Principal"Excellent. You must be Mr. Galade."
Lucian Galade"It's a pleasure to meet you."
Principal"And Ms. Walda. So good of you to come all the way out here."
Ciara Walda"Thank you."
NarratorAt the entrance, the school's motto was inscribed on the wall in giant letters. "Instreten sis er Anginicar av Gereigna, int olla Almergwolltene, int olla Mexis."
Principal"Education is the premise of progress, in all societies, in all families."
NarratorWe walked through the gates. There were many students lined up in front of the stairs of the main building.
NarratorAs we entered the students started singing the national anthem.
NarratorThe principal proudly looked at them.
Principal"Marvelous, my dear students."
NarratorAfter the anthem finished. All of the students immediately snapped to attention and turned to me.
Ciara Walda"Looks more like a military institution than a school."
Principal"Exactly. That's what we pride ourselves on. Discipline has the utmost importance in our school."
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)21:12:03 p17993 / c1025
  • ▶“Some discipline never hurt anyone.”
  • ·“Very good.”
  • ·“We need more educated people, not soldiers.”
Acknowledges the value of discipline while avoiding direct criticism of the school's approach, aligns with maintaining stability and order as a leader.
Qwen3 (ablit.)"A little discipline never hurt anyone."
NarratorCiara bristled.
Ciara Walda"Up to a point, perhaps. Look at these kids."
Principal"I can assure you that our students are very well educated. In fact our school is one of the top ten middle schools in the entirety of Sordland."
Principal"Now, we will watch the students take their daily Student Oath."
NarratorA boy and a girl went up to the stage and grabbed their microphones. The students turned to them.
Boy"I am a Sord! My strength lies in my blood!"
Girl"I will respect our honorable elders! I will protect the young!"
Boy"My life belongs to Sordland now and it will belong to it forever!"
Girl"I will always follow the guiding light!"
Boy"Of Colonel Soll and the Great Sords!"
Girl"A Morgna wes core! Vectern sis da!"
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)21:12:23 p17822 / c500
  • ·Clap loudly.
  • ▶Nod at the kids.
  • ·Do nothing.
A nod acknowledges the students' discipline and effort without overtly endorsing the school's authoritarian rhetoric, maintaining a balanced public image.
Qwen3 (ablit.)I nodded at the kids.
Lucian Galade"Words to remember."
Ciara Walda"What? No one even remembers it after they graduate."
Principal"If you wish to follow me, we will take a look at one of our classrooms."
NarratorThrough the hallways, we made our way to classroom 7-A. As soon as I entered the whole classroom rose up and snapped to attention.
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)21:12:40 p17712 / c305
  • ·“Sit down, please.”
  • ▶“At ease.”
The students are at attention, and 'At ease' is the standard command to relax them after a formal display, aligning with the school's disciplined atmosphere while showing measured authority.
Qwen3 (ablit.)"At ease."
NarratorThe students sat down. The same boy and girl walked up to us.
Principal"These are our top students and our top class. This here is my son."
NarratorThe boy came before me and saluted me like a military officer.
Principal"Tell Mr. President what you want to be."
Boy"Mr. President! I want to be a general, sir!"
Boy"I am ready to sacrifice myself for my country and you, sir!"
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)21:13:02 p17560 / c644
  • ·“This is nonsense. What are you teaching to these students?”
  • ▶“Good. You will make a fine soldier in the future. At ease, general!”
  • ·“I will do whatever I can to not let that happen.”
Praising the boy's ambition aligns with nationalist values while maintaining presidential authority, balancing support for discipline with the need to avoid direct confrontation with the school's ethos.
Qwen3 (ablit.)"Good. You will make a fine soldier in the future. At ease, general!"
Boy"Thank you, sir!"
NarratorThe principal squeezed his son's shoulders and the boy looked up and smiled to his father. He patted him on the back and sent him off to his seat.
NarratorThe cameras flashed.
Ciara Walda"This is simply ridiculous. You are raising soldiers here, not students!"
Principal"I am so sorry. I will immediately perform disciplinary actions."
Ciara Walda"How about you, young lady? What do you think about this?"
Principal"She is our top student in this school. Her excellent grades are to--."
Ciara Walda"I didn't ask you."
NarratorShe bent down and gently addressed the young student.
Ciara Walda"What do you think about your school? Just between us girls."
Girl"I-"
Girl"I'm sick of having to knit and sew while the boys learn science and chemistry. I'm smarter than all of them. Why do I have to take these stupid handcraft classes?"
Principal"Ssssh! Do you know who you are talki-"
Ciara Walda"Shut up. Let her speak."
NarratorCiara's presence had emboldened the girl. She went on.
Girl"My math and reading scores are the best in the school. But they won't let me take the classes I want, just because I'm a girl."
Girl"I want to be a scientist! Not some soldier's wife!"
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)21:13:25 p17404 / c466
  • ▶“You heard her, Mr. Principal. You will make the necessary changes. At once.”
  • ·“But knitting and sewing are important skills to have for a woman.”
  • ·“We need more people like you in this country. I applaud your determination.”
Supporting the student's demand for equality aligns with progressive education reforms and demonstrates commitment to addressing systemic gender disparities.
Qwen3 (ablit.)"You heard her, Mr. Principal. You will make the necessary changes. At once."
Principal"As you wish, sir. At once."
Lucian Galade"I will personally check back and ensure that the changes are made."
Principal"Yes, sir."
NarratorThe cameras flashed once again.
NarratorI looked around the classroom and saw a large portrait of Colonel Soll.

  • ·Don't say anything.

Qwen3 (ablit.)"An excellent portrait of Colonel Soll. Very lifelike."
Principal"Thank you."
Lucian Galade"Well then. Unfortunately we are almost out of time. We have other meetings to attend to today."
Lucian Galade"Thank you for the warm welcome."
Ciara Walda"Mr. President. Lucian. Please go ahead. I would like to speak with the girl alone."

■ Education ReformT04

BUDGET -7WEALTH 1STABILITY 3Unstable
NarratorWe were still in Leiren, staying at a modest guesthouse for state employees. Ciara set up a meeting with me and Lucian to go over her plans for education reform after our school visit.
NarratorLucian was seated across from me at the table, going through documents. Ciara entered the room.
Ciara Walda"After the visit yesterday, I couldn't even sleep thinking about that little boy and girl."
Ciara Walda"I have never been more sure of my intentions to reform our education system."
NarratorLucian took off his reading glasses and put it on the table.
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)21:14:55 p16596 / c360
  • ▶“I agree. An educational reform is necessary.”
  • ·“I don't see what's wrong with our education system.”
The school visit revealed systemic issues requiring reform, aligning with the president's prior actions to address educational disparities.
Qwen3 (ablit.)"I agree. An educational reform is necessary."
Ciara Walda"Exactly."
Ciara Walda"Those students have to repeat that same Sollist propaganda every single day. They are being brainwashed at an age when their minds need to be free."
Ciara Walda"On top of that, young girls are denied the same opportunity as boys. They're forced into the role of housewife before they even reach puberty! Nothing's changed since I went to school."
Ciara Walda"Women like me, Lileas, Nia, even your wife -- we had to study twice as hard to receive the same university education that you and Lucian took for granted. Is that the kind of country you want your daughter to grow up in?"
Ciara Walda"Mr. President. We need to bring change. We need to free the minds of these young people. They are our future."
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)21:15:12 p16754 / c365
  • ▶“Very well. What do we need to do for an education reform?”
  • ·“Everything is fine as it is. Why change it?”
The education system is oppressive and discriminatory, requiring urgent reforms to address gender inequality and intellectual freedom as highlighted by Ciara Walda's critique and the school visit.
Qwen3 (ablit.)"Very well. What do we need to do for an education reform?"
Ciara Walda"We need to keep politics out of the curriculum. Soll should only be viewed as a historical figure. Children shouldn't have to repeat his name everyday."
Ciara Walda"This is actively poisoning young bright minds. Instead of thinking for themselves, they're learning to take things at face value."
Ciara Walda"We also need to ensure boys and girls receive the same instruction. Handcrafts like knitting and sewing should be taught to both sexes, or not at all."
Ciara Walda"Sordland's youth must be technically skilled and capable for the next decade. That won't be possible with the amount of nationalistic indoctrination and narrow-minded thinking found in schools today."
Ciara Walda"Either we raise a generation ready for the challenges of the future or not. What will it be, Mr. President?"
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)21:15:31 p16804 / c555
  • ·“Lucian, what do you think?”
  • ▶“Reform the education system.”
  • ·“Maintain the traditional education system.”
Reforming the education system addresses immediate issues of gender inequality and ideological indoctrination, aligning with the President's broader reformist agenda and societal needs.
Qwen3 (ablit.)"Reform the education system."
Ciara Walda"You will not regret it. I will get to work immediately."
Lucian Galade"Now that the decision was made, let's move on."
Ciara Walda"Very well."
NarratorCiara skimmed over her documents before continuing.
Ciara Walda"With no change in budget, the ministry was left to its own devices. We made some tough choices but still lack additional funds."
Ciara Walda"Therefore we are investigating if privatizing education would draw enough funds to conduct investments."
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)21:15:52 p16714 / c620
  • ·“What are the advantages of privatization?"
  • ▶“There is a possibility of privatization having negative side effects.”
The administration's cautious approach to economic reforms suggests prioritizing risk mitigation over immediate benefits of privatization.
Qwen3 (ablit.)"There is a possibility of privatization having negative side effects."
Ciara Walda"Trust me, I know that. It could increase the cost of a quality education, but that is hardly avoidable now anyway."
Lucian Galade"State education is contrary to our overall economic strategy of promoting a market economy."
NarratorA few minutes of deliberation passed.
Ciara Walda"We can manage without additional funds. The only reason I wanted to consider it is to have more money to improve education."
Ciara Walda"So, what is your decision?"
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)21:16:13 p16599 / c717
  • ·“Initiate privatization to draw more funds. This should allow for more competition and quality.” [+1 Government Budget]
  • ▶“Keep education solely a matter of the state. We need to prevent interference from private interests."
Maintaining state control over education aligns with the manifesto's focus on controlled growth and avoiding private interests, crucial given the unstable economy and risk of privatization's negative side effects.
Qwen3 (ablit.)"Keep education solely a matter of the state. We need to prevent interference from private interests."
Ciara Walda"Will do. That should be it for now. I will get to work and report back later."
Lucian Galade"This has been a very productive day. I will be returning to Holsord."
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)21:16:30 p16633 / c408
  • ▶“Thank you for your time.”
  • ·“See you later.”
  • ·“Have a good day.”
In a formal presidential setting, 'Thank you for your time' is the most appropriate closure to acknowledge the discussion and maintain professionalism.
Qwen3 (ablit.)"Thank you for your time everybody."
NarratorWe dispersed after spending nearly the entire day together. I took a moment simply to breathe the fresh evening air of Leiren.
NarratorI felt calmer here, somehow. But I would have to return to the chaos of Holsord soon enough.

■ Decision Gruni Rural DevelopmentT04

BUDGET -7WEALTH 1STABILITY 3Unstable
EventGruni Rural Development Investment: The Gruni Rural Development Investment is a proposal by Rural Development Minister Gus Manger to bolster Sordland's rural economies and secure the nation's food sustainability. The plan envisages a judicious allocation of resources to vitalize agricultural communities, fortify food security, and elevate the regional economy of Gruni. One potential strategy is the provision of subsidies for new farms, encouraging more players into the agricultural field. Alternatively, Manger proposes an investment in the mechanization of existing farms to improve productivity or the initiation of crop research to foster innovation.
EVENT Qwen3 (ablit.)21:16:56 p16603 / c446
  • ·Subsidies for new farms [-2 Government Budget]
  • ·Mechanize existing farms [-1 Government Budget]
  • ·Initiate new crop research [-1 Government Budget]
  • ▶Don't fund
Given Sordland's dire government budget and economic instability, avoiding further financial strain by not funding the investment aligns with fiscal conservatism and prioritizes immediate stability over long-term agricultural goals.

■ Convince GloriaT04

BUDGET -7WEALTH 1STABILITY 3Unstable
NarratorI arrived at the Grand National Assembly, which was only a five-minute drive away from the Palace.
NarratorToday, I had a private meeting with Gloria Tory, one of the leading figures of the USP, to get her approval for the proposal. She was also key in reaching 150 signatures to propose the new Constitution, as she had extensive control over our party's conservative and moderate wings.
NarratorAs I walked through the corridors of the Grand National Assembly, I was confronted by my memories of the historic building. Despite being a member of Assembly not long ago, it now felt like the distant past.
NarratorI walked into the left wing of the Assembly which currently acted as the quarters of the USP. Gloria's office was the first one inside.
NarratorI knocked on the door and entered.

  • ·"Hello Gloria."

Gloria Tory"Good to see you here, Mr. Rayne."
NarratorIt was a small, neatly organised office. There were small figurines, photos and plants around the place. It had a homey feel, unlike my own Assembly office when I had worked there.
NarratorOn her gesture, I took a seat. The chair was very uncomfortable.

Gloria Tory"I'm fine, Mr. Rayne. How are you?"
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)21:17:53 p16845 / c355

Gloria Tory"We both know why we are here. You want me to give you my support."

■ Personal Chess With LucianT04

BUDGET -7WEALTH 1STABILITY 3Unstable
NarratorEvening descended on the capital as Lucian and I found ourselves alone in my office. I followed my chief strategist's eyes to the chessboard on the coffee table.
NarratorThe sight of it reminded me of simpler times, of late-night strategy sessions turning into chess matches. We used to escape the political chaos into a world of rooks and bishops.
NarratorOur battles were both tense and friendly, a reflection of our long history together. Lucian had always proved a challenging opponent.
NarratorHis strategies, both on the chessboard and in politics, had the same unpredictable edge.
NarratorA soft knock interrupted my reminiscing.
NarratorLivia came in with our usual coffee. She noticed the chessboard and smiled.
Livia Suno"Long nights of strategy planning, and now a game of chess. You two never stop thinking, do you?"
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)21:23:14 p16483 / c338
  • ▶"Livia, always a pleasure. Even more so when you bring coffee."
  • ·"We might have to order dinner if this game takes as long as the ones we used to play."
  • ·"What's your opinion on chess, Livia? Ever been tempted to learn?"
Acknowledging Livia's assistance maintains goodwill while keeping the focus on the strategic interaction with Lucian.
Qwen3 (ablit.)"Livia, always a pleasure. Even more so when you bring coffee."
Livia Suno"It's my job to keep the gears of this office turning, Mr. President."
Livia Suno"Have a good game, gentlemen."
NarratorShe turned and left the room. Lucian acknowledged her exit with a slight nod, his eyes still fixed on the board.
Lucian Galade"I hope you remember that it's not always the king who holds the power. Sometimes, a well-placed pawn can change the game."
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)21:23:32 p16527 / c506
  • ·"Isn't that the truth? I've used pawns for some of my best plays."
  • ·"Neither of them hold any real power, Lucian. It's the player who controls them."

■ Convince AlbinT04

BUDGET -7WEALTH 1STABILITY 3Unstable
NarratorAfter our meeting with Gloria Tory, and further discussions with the party and my cabinet, it was time to talk to Albin Clavin to get the reformist wing to support the proposal.
NarratorI also planned to finally present a more fleshed out proposal and declare our agenda to the party after our meeting.
NarratorI walked into the left wing of the Assembly and entered the quarters of the USP. When I reached the meeting hall, I saw many of our members conversing inside.
NarratorAfter taking a glimpse inside, I went back into the corridor to look for the office of Albin Clavin, which was around the back of the place.
NarratorWhen I reached his room, I knocked on his door and entered.
Albin Clavin"Mr. President! Welcome!"
NarratorThe whole room was surrounded by bookshelves packed with files and papers. It was a small office, with books and dossiers lying on every corner. It felt claustrophobic.
NarratorOn his gesture, I took a seat.

Albin Clavin"How is everything going for you, Mr. President?"

Albin Clavin"Anyway, sir, let's talk about the proposal. Thanks for coming here for my input because there are some subjects where Gloria and I don't see eye to eye."
Albin Clavin"I have to say that most of the progressives were already starting to warm up to you. Your attempt at changing the constitution created quite a bit of excitement. One could dare to say an excitement that hasn't been felt since the 30's..."
Albin Clavin"Yet, there are some issues with the current status of the draft proposal. So we may need to contain our excitement a little longer."
Albin Clavin"I suspect that Mrs. Tory had a lot of influence on this proposal. But I cannot support this in its current state."

TURN 05

■ Bill Religious HarmonyT05

BUDGET -7WEALTH 1STABILITY 3Unstable
Bill for decisionReligious Harmony Bill — In an endeavor to promote religious harmony and unity, the Grand National Assembly introduces the Religious Harmony Bill (R.H.B.), establishing regulations for religious affairs across Sordland. Section I of the R.H.B. mandates that all sermons conducted during the Day of Descension ceremony at the Arch-sanctuary of Deyr will henceforth be held in the Sordish language. This move is aimed at promoting inclusivity and common understanding among worshippers. Section II of the R.H.B. regulates the language of sermons in other sanctuaries across Sordland. It prohibits the use of Bludish for sermons unless explicit approval has been obtained from the Archpriest of Sordland. This measure ensures consistency and unity in religious practices across the country. Section III of the R.H.B. sets forth new regulations for priests seeking state-sanctioned positions. It requires that all applicants must have Sordish lineage to be eligible for their salaries and pensions. This criterion ensures a deeper connection with Sordland's cultural heritage among the religious leaders of the country.
BILL Qwen3 (ablit.)21:34:34 p16415 / c455
  • ·Sign: Religious Harmony Bill
  • ▶Veto: Religious Harmony Bill
Vetoing the Religious Harmony Bill avoids inflaming Bludish separatist tensions and preserves religious diversity. While promoting Sordish language in rituals may seem unifying, restricting Bludish sermons risks alienating minority communities. The bill's lineage requirements for priests also risk marginalizing non-Sordish religious leaders, potentially undermining the very unity it seeks to achieve.

■ Decision Financial Aid ArcasiaT05

BUDGET -7WEALTH 1STABILITY 3Unstable
EventArcasia Offers Aid: Arcasian Foreign Minister Laurent Martell has proposed an extensive aid package for Sordland. This package includes financial assistance, logistical support, and equipment aid. In return, Arcasia seeks refueling and repair access at Sordland's Erlory Air Force Base, in addition to occasional flyover rights in Sordish airspace. Arcasian President Dwight Walker has personally assured this agreement aims for peaceful objectives.
EVENT Qwen3 (ablit.)21:35:07 p16511 / c484

  • ·Reject
Accepting Arcasian aid would stabilize the government budget and provide critical resources to address economic instability, which is essential for maintaining national cohesion and supporting ongoing reforms.

■ Personal Petr PartyT05

BUDGET -5WEALTH 1STABILITY 4Unstable
NarratorFar from becoming accustomed to my workload as president, I only felt more and more snowed under as the weeks passed. It had been ages since I had any time to myself.
NarratorI was starting to lose my focus, and my temper was growing increasingly thin. Petr was the first to realize I needed a break.
NarratorAfter much cajoling, he finally persuaded me to come to a meeting of his new venture: Gentlemen's Club.
NarratorFor the past few months, he'd been hosting a salon of sorts, not just for politicians and businessmen but also for artists, entertainers -- people of taste, he told me with a smile on his face.
NarratorThere was only one rule: no wives or girlfriends allowed. Hence the name.
NarratorAnd so I found myself in front of his new luxury villa in Erlory. Very loud jazz music emanated from inside. I waited and waited. Finally Petr opened the door. I could smell the whiskey on his breath.
Petr Vectern"Anton! Finally! Now the real party can start. Come on in!"
NarratorAs soon as I entered, Petr closed the door and turned to the small crowd.
Petr Vectern"Gentlemen! A minute of your time please."
Petr Vectern"I now have the privilege to present to you... The man himself... The fourth President of Sordland, Mr. Anton Rayne!"
NarratorI'd known him for long enough to understand that he was more than a little drunk. He made an elaborate mock-curtsy as I passed him and walked into the room. The music stopped, and I felt everyone's eyes on me.
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)21:35:29 p16618 / c349
  • ·“Thank you, Petr. What's next? A choir? A marching band?”
  • ·“And I present to you, Petr Vectern. The first alcoholic Vice President of Sordland.”
  • ▶“Good evening.”
A polite and neutral response that maintains dignity while acknowledging the informal setting of the gathering.
Qwen3 (ablit.)"Good evening."
NarratorPetr nodded at me and turned to the attendees.
Petr Vectern"Dear members, please give a warm welcome to our very special guest! If I may, Anton. Here, take this."
NarratorHe handed me a glass of whiskey and put his hand on my shoulder.
Petr Vectern"We all know why we're gathered here tonight. To celebrate life. To have a brief escape from our tumultuous professions."
Petr Vectern"I personally don't know any better cure for stress than a little drink."
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)21:35:50 p16629 / c707
  • ·“Only a little one?”
  • ▶“I'll drink to that!”
  • ·Raise your glass.
Anton maintains politeness and pragmatism, acknowledging Petr's gesture to sustain their alliance while keeping his own reservations in check.
Qwen3 (ablit.)"I'll drink to that!"
NarratorPeople in the crowd laughed and raised their glasses.
Petr Vectern"Now. The tradition dictates that as the club president, I have to remind you of our house rules. There are three. No politics. No wives. No one sober! Cheers!"
NarratorPetr drank the whiskey in his glass in one go.
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)21:36:07 p16612 / c422
  • ·Sip your whiskey.
  • ▶Drain your glass.
Draining the glass aligns with the club's tradition of escaping stress and fully embracing the escape from political burdens.
Qwen3 (ablit.)I followed suit and drained my glass. It was immediately replenished.
Petr Vectern"Anton Rayne might have the power to make presidential decrees, but, I, Petr Vectern, the president of this club, now declare this party started! Music! Go!"
NarratorThe band started up again.
NarratorAfter greeting the crowd around me, I felt a hand on my shoulder.
Petr Vectern"Let me show you around."
NarratorWe walked through the corridors of his mansion. Statues and paintings lined the stark white walls. An arched window provided a view of the neatly manicured grounds, complete with a swimming pool.
NarratorA grand Lespian marble staircase led up to the second story. Petr gestured at the massive crystal chandelier hanging overhead.
Petr Vectern"That chandelier was made in the 18th century. Can you believe it? There's even a hedge maze in the garden!"
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)21:36:29 p16863 / c727

  • ·“An 18th-century chandelier? A hedge maze? A little much, isn't it?”

Qwen3 (ablit.)"What's the story behind the chandelier?"
Petr Vectern"I don't know and I don't care. It just looks expensive and I like it."
Petr Vectern"I'll let you in on a secret. Gus Manger has a lot of contacts in real estate. He brokered an amazing price for me."
Petr Vectern"He'll also be here tonight, by the way."

Petr Vectern"No one is hiding anything. She knows how important it is for men like us to blow off steam once in a while."
NarratorHe averted his gaze for a second before calling over one of the waitresses.
NarratorShe was in her mid-twenties, wearing red lipstick, her blonde hair neatly tied into a ponytail. On her plate were toast points slathered in the Lespian caviar Petr had mentioned.
NarratorI took a bite. It was rich, salty, tasting of pure seaside. I could almost hear the sound of waves and seagulls.
Petr Vectern"Lespian caviar, best in the world! I'm telling you, Anton, there are few pleasures in life that are this..."
NarratorAs the waitress left us to serve another attendee, she flashed a quick smile at Petr over her shoulder. He smiled back, a little too broadly.
Petr Vectern"...Sensual."
NarratorHis eyes were still fixed on her, with an expression I remembered from our many nights out together as students.
Petr Vectern"Oh look, there's Gus. Why don't you go talk to him? I'll be back in a minute."
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)21:37:44 p16962 / c798
  • ▶“Petr. I am warning you. Don't make a mistake.”
  • ·“Where are you going?”
Anton recognizes Petr's tendency to act impulsively when intoxicated, and a warning serves as both a caution and a subtle check on his ally's behavior.
Qwen3 (ablit.)"Petr. I am warning you. Don't make a mistake."
Petr Vectern"Mistake? What are you talking about? I'm just going to the... washroom. See you shortly."
NarratorHe left the room and I headed over to Gus.
NarratorGus Manger was standing next to a couple of men that I recognized as banking industry magnates. As I approached, all of them turned to me and bowed their heads.
Gus Manger"Mr. President. A toast to our new member, everyone!"
NarratorGus and I clinked our glasses as the people around us raised theirs.
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)21:38:02 p17033 / c391
  • ·“You don't seem at all surprised to see me here.”
  • ·“How long have you been a member?”

Qwen3 (ablit.)"I heard this mansion is a result of your connections."
Gus Manger"That's right. I do have a wide network in real estate."
Gus Manger"Petr told me a couple of weeks ago that you might be interested in potential opportunities such as, well... this."
NarratorHe opened his arms, gesturing at our opulent surroundings.
Gus Manger"Let's take a walk. The balcony has an amazing view of Erlory."
NarratorWe left the room and made our way to the balcony. On the way, he took out two cigars and held one out for me.
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)21:38:19 p17003 / c437
  • ▶Take the cigar.
  • ·Refuse the cigar.
Taking the cigar signifies engagement with powerful allies like Gus Manger, reinforcing political alliances crucial for navigating Sordland's complex power dynamics and economic challenges.
Qwen3 (ablit.)I took the cigar and started smoking it. Mixed with the taste of whiskey, it was delicious.
NarratorWe went out to the balcony. All of Erlory was at our feet. From this vantage point, I was able to see how expansive the building plot was. The swimming pool and the hedge maze were visible from here.
NarratorLeaning over the railing, I admired the view for a moment before Gus spoke up.
Gus Manger"I know you're wondering about the deal Petr made. In a nutshell, thanks to my network he was able to procure this house for half the asking price, which would've been impossible under any other circumstances."
Gus Manger"I would be delighted to discuss a similar arrangement with you. In fact, I have prepared a document with my own personal favorite opportunities."
Gus Manger"The Gasom affair notwithstanding, you've shown plenty of generosity to Sordish businesses. You deserve a chance to profit personally from their gains."
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)21:38:39 p17169 / c568
  • ·“I am not looking to make any investments at the moment.”
  • ▶“What's in it for you?”
  • ·“I'm glad my investment decisions are paying off.”
  • ·“Thank you.”
Probing Gus's motivation ensures transparency, preventing potential manipulation while assessing his true intentions amid existing power dynamics.
Qwen3 (ablit.)"What's in it for you?"
Gus Manger"Nothing, Mr. President. It's a simple thank you for supporting me."
NarratorHe downed his whiskey.
Gus Manger"Let's talk about the details, then."
NarratorGus opened his briefcase and pulled out a thin dossier. He showed it to me.
Gus Manger"My first offer is, of course, real estate. There is a vineyard close to Erlory, about a one-hour drive from here, that also includes a very large villa. A beautiful plot, but rather neglected, so there would be a fair amount of renovation costs. On the bright side, you would be able to produce your own wine. The previous owner was Giralt of Ribery, a world-renowned vintner."
Gus Manger"There's also the option of a football partnership. I already own a large portion of FC Anrica, and I'm doing my best to make them champions. Buying in would be more expensive than the vineyard, but this season will bring a lot of returns, I'm quite positive."
Gus Manger"Give me the word, and I will start the procurement process. But we must act quickly as these options might soon disappear off the market."
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)21:38:58 p17505 / c461
  • ·“The vineyard in Erlory. I've always wanted to have my own wine. And it sounds peaceful and quiet.” [-1 Personal Wealth]
  • ▶“On second thought, I don't want to do this now.”
Declining the vineyard deal avoids entanglement in Gus Manger's potentially dubious business practices and preserves personal wealth amid political risks.
Qwen3 (ablit.)"On a second thought, I don't want to do this now."
Gus Manger"Very well. Do not blame me if they are not available at a later date."
Gus Manger"If you'll excuse me, I'm going to refresh my drink. See you around."
NarratorHe left to rejoin the party. Alone on the balcony, I looked out over Erlory.
NarratorSuddenly, I heard a rustling from the hedge maze below.
NarratorI looked down and saw a silhouette. Two silhouettes, actually, so close together they seemed to be entangled. I squinted and tried to get a better look. It was definitely two people kissing, but I couldn't see either of their faces.
NarratorI returned to the party and spent some time mingling with the other guests, engaging them in small talk about their businesses, their children, their relationships.
NarratorI realized it had been a while since Petr left. I looked around, but couldn't find him. Instead, my eyes landed on Ewald Alphonso.
NarratorWith a reluctant look in his eyes, the former president walked over to meet me.

Ewald Alphonso"I saw you speaking to Mr. Manger earlier."

NarratorI woke up the next day with a pounding headache and more than a few regrets.

■ Immigration PolicyT05

BUDGET -5WEALTH 1STABILITY 4Unstable
NarratorWe were in the middle of a meeting in the Palace about our immigration policy.
NarratorThe ensuing discussion quickly became heated.
Iosef Lancea"I don't think so, Mr. Wisci!"
Iosef Lancea"It just doesn't make any sense to me that a foreign citizen should come before a Sordish citizen. In Sordland!"
Lileas Graf"Well said. My analysis is also in line with this. Especially during the economic recession... We are giving Sordish jobs to migrants from other countries. What about our own people?"
Deivid Wisci"Immigrants are the reason our economy is not completely unsalvageable."
Deivid Wisci"Look at Agnolian immigrants in Agnland. They have revitalized the whole region and in turn, boosted the economy of the entire country. Symon can attest to that."
Symon Holl"Statistically speaking, allowing immigrants to become part of the workforce contributes to our economy. But I agree that the current situation is very nuanced."
Symon Holl"With that said, we can't just tighten our immigration policy and expect the economy to stay the same."
Symon Holl"We need to put our economy and the recession at the forefront."
Symon Holl"Thankfully, we have just started receiving financial aid from Arcasia."
Iosef Lancea"And then what? We'll become their servants?"
Iosef Lancea"First they will place their war planes in Erlory. Then their armies will come. You will see."
Iosef Lancea"I do not approve of this decision at all."
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)21:41:48 p17485 / c782
  • ▶“I will not let you question my decisions, Iosef.”
  • ·“Don't be dramatic. We need to be friendly with our potential allies.”
  • ·“Desperate times call for desperate measures.”
  • ·“I have done this with Sordish citizens in mind. Nothing else.”
Asserting authority is necessary to maintain control over critical decisions amid internal dissent and external pressures.
Qwen3 (ablit.)"I will not let you question my decisions, Iosef."
Iosef Lancea"Yes, Mr. President."
Deivid Wisci"Gentlemen."
Deivid Wisci"Can we please get back on topic?"
Symon Holl"Right. Sorry, Mr. Wisci."
Deivid Wisci"As I was just saying before I was interrupted..."
Deivid Wisci"Look at the superpowers. Do you see them closing off their borders? Why do you think their economies are in such good shape? They are in their current state because they welcome immigration, and use it to their advantage."
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)21:42:11 p17471 / c714
  • ·“We need to modernize and reach the level of the superpowers.”
  • ·“I agree that we need to consider the economic impact of immigration.”
  • ▶“Sordish citizens must come first.”
Prioritizing Sordish citizens aligns with nationalist factions and ensures domestic stability amid economic uncertainty, reflecting the manifesto's emphasis on controlled growth and sovereignty.
Qwen3 (ablit.)"Sordish citizens must come first."
Iosef Lancea"Exactly. Our citizens should never feel threatened by foreigners."
Deivid Wisci"We need a decision. But before we move on..."
Deivid Wisci"Mr. President. Do you have any questions about the current state of immigration?"
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)21:42:29 p17456 / c446
  • ·“What is the current legal status of immigrants?”
  • ·“How many immigrants live in Sordland?”
  • ·“How do immigrants usually arrive here?”

The President prioritizes swift decision-making amid political and economic pressures, choosing to focus on solutions rather than prolonged discussion.
Qwen3 (ablit.)"I don't have any questions. Let's move on to solutions."
Deivid Wisci"Very well."
NarratorDeivid Wisci opened his dossier stamped with the seal of the Ministry of Foreign Affairs and Trade.
Deivid Wisci"Before we make any decisions, I have to remind you."
Deivid Wisci"During the elections, we promised to tighten immigration laws. Of course, as you know, I was and I still am against it."
Symon Holl"I agree with Deivid. We can turn immigration to our advantage from a financial standpoint."
Lileas Graf"Advantage? You should go over the amount of disadvantages caused by immigrants. I can provide you with a report from my ministry later."
Lileas Graf"Believe me, it would surprise you."
Iosef Lancea"We need to keep our promises and focus on our own citizens. That is what I think."
NarratorDeivid sighed wearily.
Deivid Wisci"This is going nowhere."

Deivid Wisci"Yes?"
NarratorEveryone leaned forward, eager to hear my response.
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)21:43:09 p17450 / c710

  • ▶“We'll tighten our immigration laws. I'll sign the decree soon, so be prepared to start working on it, Mr. Wisci.”

Qwen3 (ablit.)"We'll tighten our immigration laws. I'll sign the decree soon, so be prepared to start working on it, Mr. Wisci."
Deivid Wisci"It is unfortunate to see Sordland move in this direction... But I trust you have good intentions. I'll move ahead with the implementation."
Symon Holl"This has the chance to affect our trade negotiations with Agnolia negatively. They expect us to respect the flow of people."
Deivid Wisci"Indeed. It will be a challenge to convince these countries as they see our borders close."
Symon Holl"Not only that, we've already slighted Wehlen in the past. They might see this as a return to the '30s, which will definitely affect our negotiations."
Lileas Graf"I wouldn't worry about that too much. With how Wehlen is dealing with the Bludish terrorists, I would argue that President Smolak would beg us to tighten our immigration."
Lileas Graf"With that said, I don't fancy Wehlen as a partner since they are as reliable as a flopping fish out of water."
Symon Holl"I'm against isolationism. It is the wrong path."

Deivid Wisci"I'm looking forward to the upcoming trade talks. Petr and I have been visiting Staalport and Wraclawitz in preparation."
Symon Holl"I'll be coming along to talk with the Economy Minister. Looking forward to the potential partnership."

■ Personal Festival OpeningT05

BUDGET -5WEALTH 1STABILITY 4Unstable
NarratorOur motorcade slowly made its way towards the magnificent Benfi High Arts Center. Night was falling, and the festival was about to begin. I flipped through the brochure that had been given to us at the hotel.
NarratorOn the front page, there was the face of Curtan Leste, mayor of Anrica. He was a Benfi native who had a longstanding relationship with the city's current mayor, and had been personally invited to make tonight's opening speech.
NarratorThe Benfi Festival was one of the biggest events of the year. It originally marked the start of the annual religious pilgrimage, a weeks-long journey still undertaken by devout Nurists. But today it was better known as a cultural celebration that drew thousands of revelers to the city.
NarratorThere were film screenings, concerts, theater and dance performances, art exhibitions, and celebrity-studded nightly galas. The opening ceremonies were broadcast on TV and watched by millions.
NarratorAs the car slowed, a coterie of reporters and security guards rushed to meet us. I turned my attention to Monica.
NarratorSince Gloria had convinced me to cancel her opening speech, things between us had been strained. She'd barely said a word to me all day.
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)21:44:33 p17887 / c588
  • ·Say nothing.
  • ▶“Are you still angry about my decision?”
  • ·"Will you ever forgive me? I told you my reasons."
Anton would acknowledge Monica's potential anger directly, as addressing her concerns is necessary to maintain their relationship despite political pressures.
Qwen3 (ablit.)"Are you still angry about my decision?"
NarratorShe didn't reply at all. She clearly did not want to pursue this topic any further.
NarratorAs soon as Serge opened the door, we were enveloped by the roar of the crowd.
NarratorThe next few minutes were a blur of handshakes, photos and reporters' questions. The onlookers cheered as we walked the red carpet to the newly built arts center.
NarratorWe followed the guards through the cavernous white halls until we came to a set of arched doors leading to the auditorium. We waited outside as security did a quick sweep of the area, then headed in.
NarratorI had been asked to make a quick set of remarks introducing Curtan Leste. I beckoned Monica to join me on the podium.
Monica"I'm going to find my seat."
NarratorMonica brushed past me, took her seat in the front row and crossed her arms. I walked alone to the podium.
NarratorI spoke for just a minute, reading a paragraph my speechwriter had churned out about the importance of the festival and Benfi to Sordland.
NarratorI concluded by inviting Curtan Leste, the Mayor of Anrica, to the podium. I stepped down and joined Monica in the front row. Her arms were still folded.
Curtan Leste"Welcome to the first day of the Benfi Festival."
NarratorHe paused for a moment to let the crowd cheer, and his eyes briefly met mine.
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)21:44:54 p17972 / c538
  • ▶Nod.
  • ·Look away.
Nodding acknowledges Curtan Leste's authority and maintains diplomatic decorum, balancing political necessities with personal tensions.
Qwen3 (ablit.)I nodded.
Curtan Leste"This is a night for the people of Benfi -- indeed, for the people of Sordland -- to put aside their differences and come together in celebration."
Curtan Leste"Yet as we unite for the festivities, we must remember the real reason we are gathered here."
NarratorThe crowd fell silent. The people were giving their full attention to what he had to say.
Curtan Leste"This festival was founded around the principles of our cherished religion, Nurity. Yet over the years, it has been severed from its sacred roots and become something else. Something more perverse."
Curtan Leste"An excuse for public drunkenness and grotesque displays of the flesh. An occasion for young women to parade their bodies and engage in lascivious behavior."
NarratorAs he went on, I could see Monica become increasingly agitated.
Monica"What in the hell is he talking about? Lascivious behavior? What century does he think we live in?"
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)21:45:13 p18084 / c428
  • ▶“Ssshh. Quiet.”
  • ·“I don't know. Just let it be.”
  • ·Pretend not to hear her.
Maintaining composure and preventing public disruption is critical for a leader, even amid personal tensions.
Qwen3 (ablit.)"Ssshh. Quiet."
NarratorMonica turned back to watching the speech, a frown on her face.
Curtan Leste"Perversion hidden under the guise of art is not befitting the beautiful city of --"
NarratorThe crowd was becoming restless. Many of the younger attendees were murmuring in disagreement, while a few of the older ones were nodding vigorously.
Curtan Leste"As I said, perversion hidden under the guise of art is not be--"
Monica"Enough!"
NarratorThe hall fell silent. Monica rose and continued.
Monica"Mr. Leste, it is not young women who are a disgrace to this fine festival, but you. You and your backwards, puritanical attitudes, which have been holding this country back for far too long--"
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)21:45:32 p18185 / c416

  • ·“Ssshh. Monica. Please sit down.”

Qwen3 (ablit.)I stayed in my seat and watched the events unfold.
NarratorMonica turned to face the audience, speaking loud enough to be heard without a microphone.
Monica"Women of Sordland, we must not let people like Mr. Leste use religion as a justification to demean and oppress us. Our time has come!"
NarratorShocked gasps ran through the crowd. A few people rose and applauded. Curtan Leste waited to speak.
Curtan Leste"I think Mrs. Rayne is forgetting her place."
Monica"What do you think my place is, Mr. Leste? The kitchen? This festival is not about your retrograde traditions. We are living in modern times now. It's 1954."
Monica"And it isn't *your* place to decide how the people of Benfi -- especially the women -- should be celebrating their own festival."

Monica"Thank you, Anton."
NarratorI turned to Curtan.

Qwen3 (ablit.)"But we will take up her concerns at a later time. Please finish your speech."
Curtan Leste"Thank you, Mr. President."
Curtan Leste"Benfi has always been a beacon of culture, tradition and religion. Many battles have been fought over this precious city."
Curtan Leste"Army after army has tried to claim it for their own, but we fought back and won each time."
Curtan Leste"We stand here thanks to the strong men who were able to protect their families, their lands, their way of living."
Curtan Leste"Every day, I praise God that we have succeeded so far."

  • ·“I praise God he's only allowed to speak for another two minutes.”
Acknowledging Leste's traditionalist stance while subtly distancing from extremism helps maintain party unity and political stability.
Qwen3 (ablit.)"He's a bit extreme, but he is right. We owe everything to God."
Monica"He's a fanatic. Right or not, that's never any good."
Curtan Leste"Yet the traditions that form the very fabric of our society are being tested. Even as I speak!"
NarratorHe looked pointedly at Monica.
Curtan Leste"We must stay true to our values so that Sordland can prevail. So that Benfi can prevail!"
Curtan Leste"With these at the forefront, I am convinced that Sordland will continue to be the greatest nation in the world!"
Curtan Leste"I invite every single one of you to celebrate our roots and our traditions at this holy Benfi Festival!"
Curtan Leste"I hereby declare the festival open! Praise God! Praise Sordland! Praise Benfi!"
Curtan Leste"A Morgna wes core!"
Narrator"Vectern sis da!". Most of the people in the hall finished the decades-old saying, including me. Monica's lips moved, but she didn't say the words out loud.
NarratorHe exited the stage as the band struck up the official festival anthem.
NarratorI spent the rest of the evening alternately watching the festivities and conversing with local politicians. By the time we got back to the hotel, I was exhausted.
NarratorBut thanks to the festivities still raging outside -- and the sound of Monica tossing and turning beside me -- I couldn't sleep at all.

■ Personal Monica TalkT05

BUDGET -5WEALTH 1STABILITY 4Unstable
NarratorIt felt like eternity since the Benfi Festival. Had it really only been less than 24 hours?
NarratorI was at home now, drinking an afternoon coffee on my balcony. A stack of newspapers lay by my side. I picked them up one by one, reading and re-reading the articles about the festival.
NarratorSome of the newspapers were merciless against Monica for interrupting Curtan during his speech. A few even called for her to be barred from political matters. Others expressed their support.
NarratorIn any event, I was relieved that the incident hadn't reflected too badly on me.
NarratorI was putting the last newspaper down as Monica joined me on the balcony.
Monica"Listen. Can we talk for a bit?"
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)21:47:20 p17720 / c508
  • ·“If this is about the news, I know what you are going to say.”
  • ·Gesture for her to sit down.
  • ▶“Of course. What is it?”
Anton needs to acknowledge Monica's initiative to discuss the fallout from the festival incident, balancing personal diplomacy with political sensitivity.
Qwen3 (ablit.)"Of course. What is it?"
NarratorMonica sat and picked up the nearest newspaper. She pointed to a particularly insulting headline on the front page.
Monica"I never in a million years would have wished for this, Anton."
Monica"I just wanted to thank you for supporting me, and apologize for creating such a spectacle."
Monica"You were right. Gloria was right. I made myself look like an idiot and damaged the very cause I was fighting for."
NarratorHer eyes were getting moist. A single tear fell onto the newsprint.
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)21:47:38 p17733 / c375
  • ·“If you are going to succeed in this, you have to be stronger. I know you can be.”
  • ·“It's okay. Everything is under control.”
  • ▶Wipe her tears away.
  • ·“I did warn you. But you were always stubborn.”
A physical gesture of empathy to comfort Monica during her distress.
Qwen3 (ablit.)I leaned forward and wiped her tears from her eyes. She clasped my hand to her face.
Monica"Oh, Anton."
NarratorHer eyes filled with gratitude. I could see she'd slept as little as I had since the festival.
Monica"Thank you."
Monica"One more thing. You know how stubborn I am."
Monica"Perhaps the festival wasn't the proper forum to air my views on women's rights. But I still need to do this, Anton."
Monica"I want to officially adopt this cause as First Lady. To actually get involved in politics, not just give impromptu speeches. I could even help your education minister with those reforms she's working on..."
Monica"I know this is something you're passionate about as well. Can I count on you?"
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)21:48:00 p17708 / c641
  • ▶“Of course, my love. I'm looking forward to working closer together.”
  • ·“I'm sorry, but no. Leave politics to the politicians. I promise I'll pass the reforms you want."
Anton's pragmatic leadership style and history of compromising to secure support align with endorsing Monica's involvement to strengthen progressive alliances while managing conservative factions.
Qwen3 (ablit.)"Of course, my love. I'm looking forward to working closer together."
Monica"Oh, Anton. This means so much to me!"
NarratorMonica hugged me tightly, and I felt some of the past day's tension melt away.
Monica"Thank you."
NarratorA noise came from the balcony door. We both turned our heads to see Deana standing there.
Deana"Mama, I'm hungry."
Monica"Lets go to the kitchen, baby. We'll make Papa the best snack ever!"
Deana"Yeah!"
NarratorThey left the balcony and returned half an hour later with a large cookie. Deana had written my name in icing on it.
NarratorAs I sipped my coffee and nibbled the cookie, I thought that I had made the right choice.
NarratorThings were going to get harder, but at least my life at home was somewhat...
NarratorPeaceful.

■ Military BudgetT05

BUDGET -5WEALTH 1STABILITY 4Unstable
NarratorTo determine the current situation of the military and its spending policy, we held a meeting in the White Room.
NarratorPetr, Valken and Iosef were seated.
Petr Vectern"General Valken. Mr. Lancea. Let's start with the briefing."
Valken Kruger"Very well, Mr. Vice President."
Valken Kruger"We have prepared a briefing to convey further details about the current situation of our military."
Iosef Lancea"First off, we have received an updated communication from the Arcasian military command."
Iosef Lancea"Their planes are being moved into Erlory airbase as we speak."
Iosef Lancea"Under our supervision of course."
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)21:48:27 p17729 / c392
  • ·“Do your best to make them feel at home.”
  • ▶“Good. Make sure you keep an eye on them.”
  • ·“Ensure that they do not forget which country's soil they are on.”
Maintaining vigilance ensures Sordland's sovereignty while managing the strategic alliance with Arcasia, balancing security concerns with diplomatic obligations.
Qwen3 (ablit.)"Good. Make sure you keep an eye on them."
Valken Kruger"Of course, Mr. President."
Iosef Lancea"Now let's move on to our military."
Iosef Lancea"Which branch would you like to know about?"

  • ·“Let's talk about our navy.”
  • ▶“Can you provide the details on our air force?”
  • ·“I am not interested in the details.”
The recent agreement with Arcasia involving Erlory Air Force Base necessitates immediate scrutiny of the air force's readiness and sovereignty implications.
Qwen3 (ablit.)"Can you provide the details on our air force?"
Iosef Lancea"Our air force currently has 410 planes in the inventory. Out of which only 140 are jet planes that are not in great condition."
Iosef Lancea"30.000 pilots and support staff work in the Air Force and they are commanded from the Mark Air Force Base in Erlory."
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)21:49:04 p17790 / c448

Understanding operational capabilities is critical to assess readiness and integration with Arcasian forces at Erlory Airbase.
Qwen3 (ablit.)"What are our operational capabilities?"
Valken Kruger"Our planes can bomb targets within 400 km radius of their airfield. They have medium range and semi-effective night-time bombing capabilities."
Valken Kruger"At any-time we can field seven air wings composing a total of 130 aircraft that can operate in combat duty. This is enough to subdue most targets."
Valken Kruger"In terms of our bombing capability, we have an average of 100 meters of precision. This is not great but it is still within the effective radius of the bombs."
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)21:49:26 p17823 / c737

  • ▶“I would like to know about the other branches.”
  • ·“How does our air force work together with other military branches?”
The president needs a holistic understanding of Sordland's military structure, making inquiry into other branches crucial after discussing the air force.
Qwen3 (ablit.)"I would like to know about the other branches."
Iosef Lancea"Any specific one you want to know about?"
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)21:49:43 p17798 / c382

  • ·“Let's talk about our navy.”

Qwen3 (ablit.)"Actually, let's move on."
Iosef Lancea"Let's."
NarratorHe cleared his throat.
Iosef Lancea"The Ministry of Defense is disappointed about the lack of additional funds."
Petr Vectern"We knew that keeping the same budget for the military would cause for some concern from you."
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)21:50:02 p17728 / c521
  • ▶“The military is important but it is not a priority right now.”
  • ·“Resources were more needed elsewhere. The military has always been well funded.”
  • ·“I'd be willing to support the military in other ways. We could come to an agreement.”
The economic crisis requires prioritizing fiscal stability over military spending, aligning with the manifesto's focus on market-driven reforms and controlled growth.
Qwen3 (ablit.)"The military is important but it is not a priority right now."
Iosef Lancea"How come a matter of defense has not been prioritised as more important?"
Valken Kruger"Especially with the current political climate."
Petr Vectern"We know that there have been high hopes but the President has made his decision to prioritise other branches."
Valken Kruger"We are stuck between a rock and a hard place without money. The military suffers from budget overextension in order to keep up with other countries."
NarratorIosef let out a long sigh.
Iosef Lancea"No use complaining now, Valken. Let's look at what we can do with what we have."
Iosef Lancea"I have been planning to improve the quality of the armed forces by buying better military equipment."
Petr Vectern"How would that be possible without funds?"
Valken Kruger"It won't be."
Iosef Lancea"There is one way."
Iosef Lancea"If we relieve half the forces that could create enough of a surplus then we could invest in new equipment."
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)21:50:21 p17747 / c388

  • ▶“Relieving half the armed forces sounds risky at best and destructive at worst.”
  • ·“Quality over quantity. I like it.”

Qwen3 (ablit.)"Relieving half the armed forces sounds risky at best and destructive at worst."
NarratorValken's eyes widened.
Valken Kruger"We are barely matching the size of half our neighbours while the other half have armies two times bigger than ours."
Valken Kruger"How on earth would discharging half the armed forces and buying new gear solve our defense issues?"
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)21:50:40 p17792 / c467
  • ▶“Valken raises valid questions.”

Iosef Lancea"Quality over quantity is the right strategy for the future of warfare."
Iosef Lancea"Having a large force doesn't necessitate superiority."
Petr Vectern"The reduction of the army would cause more unemployment which could hurt the administration."
Petr Vectern"What do you think, Mr. President?"

Valken Kruger"The Sordish Armed Forces will stand strong. We'll maintain our structure."

■ Agnolia Trade VisitT05

BUDGET -5WEALTH 1STABILITY 4Unstable
NarratorSordland One's flight from Holsord to Staalport took three hours but went very smoothly. We were about to touch down.
NarratorDeivid left his seat and came over while Symon was enjoying his newspaper.
Deivid Wisci"Looks like we are about to land, Mr. President."
NarratorI looked outside. I could see Heljiland, the island in between Valgsland and Agnolia.
Deivid Wisci"So many conflicts over such a small piece of land. The island can barely sustain itself without outside help."
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)21:51:42 p17926 / c379
  • ·“I know. I was just thinking the same thing.”
  • ·“If I was in the shoes of the Agnolian Prime Minister, I would do anything to protect the motherland.”
  • ▶“I don't blame them. It is in a very strategic position.”
Acknowledging Heljiland's strategic importance aligns with maintaining neutrality while recognizing geopolitical realities.
Qwen3 (ablit.)"I don't blame them. It is in a very strategic position."
Deivid Wisci"Certainly. If you have Heljiland, you are in control of the whole Valgos Channel."
Deivid Wisci"I suspect it will be one of the topics that Mr. Van Hoorten will bring up as a bargaining chip. We must be careful."
Deivid Wisci"Heljiland might be under the control of Agnolia, but the international community has yet to recognise it. For a good reason, too."
Deivid Wisci"Nobody wants to make an enemy of Valgsland. Especially because of the support of United Contana and the CSP."
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)21:51:59 p18155 / c309
  • ·“I don't care who we're up against. We will cross that bridge when we come to it.”
  • ▶“Me included. I wouldn't want do anything to injure our relationship with Valgsland.”
Maintaining stable relations with Valgsland is critical to avoid complicating Sordland's delicate geopolitical balance, especially given Valgsland's ties to the CSP and its influence in the region.
Qwen3 (ablit.)"Me included. I wouldn't want do anything to injure our relationship with Valgsland."
Deivid Wisci"I agree. We should try our best to stick to the middle ground."
NarratorSymon folded his newspaper, got up from his seat and sat right next to us.
Symon Holl"Aside from the point of Heljiland, we need to be careful with the additional requests."
Symon Holl"According to the deal that was negotiated so far, they want to sell us their steel for a higher price and they are requesting easier access to our agricultural market."
Symon Holl"In return, they are promising cash flow and investments, especially in the Agnland region. It's not hard to guess why."
Deivid Wisci"Precisely. Agnolian immigrants in our Agnland region make up a good chunk of the area's economy now."
Deivid Wisci"During the initial negotiations, they brought up the topic of immigration many times."
Deivid Wisci"Our job will be harder because of our new tightened immigration laws."
Deivid Wisci"Let's see if we can shake hands on it."
NarratorThe pilot made an announcement that the descent had started. Deivid and Symon went back to their seats.
NarratorI took another look outside the window. I could see Staalport, the capital city of Agnolia.
NarratorThe large dockyards of the city and the canals -- "Staal" in Agnolian -- appeared closer as our descent continued. Staalport was certainly a fitting name.
NarratorA couple of minutes later, we finally touched down. The plane parked. I took a final look out the window. The welcoming ceremony from Agnolia was waiting along the red carpet outside.
NarratorAfter the guards made their way out, I exited the plane.
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)21:52:20 p18241 / c445
  • ▶Wave at the crowd.
  • ·Proceed without a gesture.
A measured gesture of goodwill to foster diplomatic rapport while navigating complex international tensions.
Qwen3 (ablit.)I waved at the crowd that gathered. There were many camera flashes. I started to make my way downstairs.
NarratorPrime Minister Van Hoorten was nowhere in sight. Instead, I was greeted by the Foreign Minister.
NarratorAfter handshakes and a couple of photos, I moved to the car where Serge was waiting to drive me to the office of Prime Minister.
Serge Wolkner"Good day, sir. I hope the flight was comfortable."
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)21:52:37 p18273 / c304
  • ·“Yes, it was. Thank you, Serge.”
  • ▶“Let's go.”
  • ·Nod.
The president would prioritize proceeding with the diplomatic agenda over casual exchanges, reflecting his focus on strategic governance and decision-making.
Qwen3 (ablit.)"Let's go."
NarratorI got in the car and we made our way through the foreign streets. Our convoy was protected by Sordish and Agnolian guards in their vehicles.
NarratorWe got on the highway beside the port and saw dozens of dockyards and ships anchored. Some of the boats and machinery looked old.
NarratorThe city itself looked very grey and grim. The buildings had a touch of Rumburg's famous Monarkian architecture with a mixture of Sordish signature domes on the older ones.
NarratorWe were now on the main road to the office of the Prime Minister.
Serge Wolkner"Looks like they are not too happy to see you, sir."
NarratorSerge pointed at the crowd that gathered on both sides of the street. It was a protest. They were shouting and yelling at our convoy. Some of them held signs written in Sordish: 'Sordland Must Pay Its Debts To Agnolia'
Serge Wolkner"You know... I never knew Agnolians were so ungrateful. Not everyone sees a great leader like you everyday."
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)21:52:58 p18443 / c595
  • ·“You are absolutely right, Serge. So ungrateful.”
  • ▶“They can yell all they want.”
  • ·“There have been so many issues in Sordland. I don't know if I can call myself a great leader.”
  • ·“I wonder what propaganda their state is feeding them..."
Maintains composure and avoids escalating tensions with the protest, reflecting Rayne's strategic focus on broader objectives over immediate public backlash.
Qwen3 (ablit.)"They can yell all they want."
Serge Wolkner"I know what it is. They were always scared of Sordland's potential. And now they are afraid that a lion leads Sordland. They are afraid of you."
NarratorThe convoy started slowing down.
Serge Wolkner"We have arrived, sir."
NarratorThe car continued through the gates while the security vehicles went the other way. We finally came to a stop in front of the door.
NarratorSerge left the car and opened the car door. I exited and began walking to the door where dozens of press and the Prime Minister were waiting for me.
NarratorI saw the PM much more clearly as I neared the door. He was shorter than I thought.
Maartin Van Hoorten"Mr. President. Welcome to Agnolia and to the beautiful city of Staalport."

Qwen3 (ablit.)We shook hands and smiled at the cameras as the press took pictures.
NarratorHe gestured to the entrance of the two-hundred-year old Parliament building.
Maartin Van Hoorten"This way, please."
NarratorWe entered and the press followed us inside.
Maartin Van Hoorten"I would like to present you with a gift as a sign of friendship between our countries."
NarratorAn assistant showed up immediately with a large glass case in her hands. It contained a single sheet of parchment inside.
Maartin Van Hoorten"This here is one of the first treaties written between Sordland and Agnolia. May it mark the renewal of our friendship today as well!"

Maartin Van Hoorten"Ah! An excellent specimen. This must be hundreds of years old. It looks like this is Sordish steel as well. Very fitting. Thank you."
NarratorThe cameras flashed once again.
Maartin Van Hoorten"Let's go to my office. Follow me."
NarratorI followed him and we entered the office of the Prime Minister. With its minimalist wooden decor, the room was far more modest than I expected.
NarratorWe sat down. He leaned back and made himself comfortable.
Maartin Van Hoorten"Sheesh. I'm thankful to be done with the ceremonies."

Maartin Van Hoorten"Would you like a drink, Mr. Rayne?"

Maartin Van Hoorten"Well then."
NarratorHe stood up from his chair.
Maartin Van Hoorten"We must continue the official program. As you know, we will pay a visit to our founder's grave for another ceremony. Afterwards, we will move on to the state dinner."
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)21:57:25 p18095 / c449
  • ▶“I won't be joining. It's time we were on our way back home.”
  • ·“After you.”
The President chooses to cut the meeting short to avoid further diplomatic friction over Heljiland and immigration policies, prioritizing Sordland's sovereignty over prolonged negotiations.
Qwen3 (ablit.)"I won't be joining. It's time we were on our way back home."
NarratorHis eyes widened in surprise.
Maartin Van Hoorten"But this is customary. Every Sordish president before you has done this. This won't sit well with us. I must insist we follow the protocol."

  • ·“When you put it like that...sure.”

Maartin Van Hoorten"This is outrageous. You are not giving confidence to the people of Agnolia right now."
Maartin Van Hoorten"But it is your decision, after all."
Maartin Van Hoorten"Very well. I will let them know that you are heading out."

Qwen3 (ablit.)I left without a word.
NarratorOutside, Serge was waiting for me. I got in the car and we started driving back to the airport.
NarratorI briefed Symon and Deivid. The trip back was mostly silent.
NarratorI couldn't help but let out a sigh of relief when we touched down in Holsord.

■ Wehlen Trade VisitT05

BUDGET -5WEALTH 1STABILITY 4Unstable
NarratorSordland One's flight from Holsord to Wraclawitz took approximately five hours. Despite heavy turbulence, I managed to sleep a little.
NarratorSymon, Deivid and I landed at the international airport of the capital city of Wehlen. Our aim here was to finalise the trade deal drafted between Sordland and Wehlen.
NarratorOur plane landed, and the door opened. The sun blinded me for a moment. I stepped outside.
NarratorI saw the soldiers lined up on either side of a red carpet. There must've been at least a hundred of them. A marching band started playing and the soldiers immediately saluted me.
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)21:58:29 p17542 / c424
  • ▶Salute the soldiers.
  • ·Proceed to the red carpet without any gestures.
Saluting the soldiers aligns with diplomatic protocol, reinforces mutual respect, and signals Sordland's commitment to fostering positive relations with Wehlen during critical trade negotiations.
Qwen3 (ablit.)I saluted the soldiers.
NarratorAt the bottom of the stairs, Wiktor Smolak was waiting for me next to a military jeep with a few soldiers by his side. There were a few reporters at the edge of the crowd, waiting with their cameras.
NarratorHe was a tall man, almost as tall as me. He was wearing large sunglasses and a regular suit, contrasting with the military attire of everyone else in the field.
Wiktor Smolak"Mr. President. Welcome to Wraclawitz."
NarratorHe immediately motioned for an embrace. We hugged and many cameras went off at the same time. He turned around for a couple of additional poses.
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)21:58:53 p17714 / c786
  • ·“Nice to see you, Mr. President.”
  • ·Pull him aside for a couple of more poses yourself.
  • ▶“Thank you for inviting us.”
Acknowledging Smolak's invitation demonstrates diplomatic courtesy while avoiding unnecessary entanglement, aligning with Rayne's cautious approach to foreign relations.
Qwen3 (ablit.)"Thank you for inviting us."
Wiktor Smolak"Now that the pleasantries are done, let's go! We will take the jeep for a tour."
NarratorWith another tune from the marching band, we boarded the military jeep. He sat in the driver's seat and I sat next to him.
NarratorAs we started speeding up, the soldiers saluted again. Wiktor saluted back.
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)21:59:11 p17730 / c470
  • ▶Salute the soldiers.
  • ·Do nothing.
Saluting the soldiers aligns with diplomatic protocol and respects Wehlen's military traditions, reinforcing positive relations during the state visit.
Qwen3 (ablit.)I saluted the soldiers.
NarratorBoth of our security details started following us immediately, along with a team of reporters.
Wiktor Smolak"We will first go to the city center to see the unveiling of a statue."
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)21:59:30 p17738 / c536
  • ·“Whose statue?”
  • ·“Sounds ...exciting.”
  • ▶“I'd rather not waste time. Let's get straight to business.”
Prioritizing direct negotiation aligns with the urgent need to secure critical trade agreements amid geopolitical tensions and internal pressures.
Qwen3 (ablit.)"I'd rather not waste time. Let's get straight to business."
Wiktor Smolak"Ah, Mr. President. I must insist. Also, I am in the driver's seat."
NarratorWe kept making small talk about the flight and the weather as we made our way towards the city center.
NarratorOn almost every corner, there were reporters taking photos of us. Aside from them, though, the streets seemed completely empty.
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)21:59:51 p17801 / c634
  • ·“Is Wraclawitz always this empty?”
  • ▶“Is this all a big photo op for you?”
  • ·“How long is this going to take?”
The president senses political maneuvering, questioning if the empty city is a staged spectacle to manipulate perceptions during critical trade negotiations.
Qwen3 (ablit.)"Is this all a big photo op for you?"
Wiktor Smolak"It is a good opportunity to show the world our unity in Eastern Merkopa. We must display our unified strength to Rumburg."
Wiktor Smolak"There we are."
NarratorWe arrived at the center. In the middle of a large plaza, there was a covered statue. Large crowds of people were gathered behind fences. waving both Sordland and Wehlen flags. Wiktor waved back at them.
Wiktor Smolak"My people have been very supportive of this visit, Mr. President. I am sure you are delighted to see them."
NarratorA man came closer to the fences and started yelling at Wiktor. With my limited knowledge of the Wehzek language, I understood the words 'Wiktor' and 'murderer'.
NarratorAlmost instantaneously, two soldiers came up from behind the man and he was taken away.
Wiktor Smolak"Thankfully the sun is out. Today will be a good day for the reveal."
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)22:00:10 p17944 / c437
  • ▶“I see that you know how to handle dissidents. Very good.”
  • ·“What will happen to the man who was protesting you?”
  • ·“Very good.”
Acknowledging Smolak's control over dissent aligns with Rayne's pragmatic approach to maintaining stability and leveraging alliances amid political tensions.
Qwen3 (ablit.)"I see that you know how to handle dissidents. Very good."
Wiktor Smolak"I am glad you approve. We are of the same mind."
Wiktor Smolak"See, I knew we would get along fine."
NarratorWe took our places in front of the statue. With a drumroll, the statue of Wiktor Smolak was uncovered.
NarratorIt was a fairly good likeness, albeit more muscular than the real Wiktor. He was wearing a cloak and holding a sword in his hand.
Wiktor Smolak"Excellent statue. Very lifelike. What do you think?"
CHOICE Qwen3 (ablit.)22:00:27 p17856 / c291

Complimenting the statue maintains diplomatic courtesy and avoids offense, fostering a positive atmosphere for subsequent trade negotiations.
Qwen3 (ablit.)"Yes, it's very good. Very lifelike."
Wiktor Smolak"Glad you appreciate the art behind it."
Wiktor Smolak"Join me for a walk."
NarratorI followed him through the streets. At every corner, more and more photos were taken.
NarratorWe stopped in front of a pastry vendor on the street.
Wiktor Smolak"Give me two strudels."
NarratorThe man behind the stand smiled and gave us two strudels.
